topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL607 - PCECL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Photodiode**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photodiode** is a specialized semiconductor p-n junction device that converts incident optical (light) energy into measurable electrical current through the **internal photoelectric effect**. It operates under **reverse bias**, where the depletion region is widened, and photon-generated electron-hole pairs are swept apart by the strong electric field, producing a photocurrent proportional to the incident light intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A photodiode is a reverse-biased p-n junction diode in which the reverse current is governed by the rate at which electron-hole pairs are generated by photons absorbed in or near the depletion region. The resulting current flows in the reverse direction and is directly proportional to the incident optical power.*

**Key Operating Modes of a Photodiode:**
1. **Photovoltaic Mode (Zero Bias):** Used in solar cells and light meters; linear but low speed.
2. **Photoconductive Mode (Reverse Bias):** Used in high-speed optical communication; fast response, higher linearity.
3. **Avalanche Photodiode (APD) Mode:** Internal gain through impact ionization; used in low-light detection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Rain Gauge on a Roof" Analogy:** Imagine a slanted rooftop with a narrow gutter. Raindrops (photons) falling on the roof trickle down into the gutter. The water collected in the gutter flows out through a pipe (the external circuit). The harder the rain (brighter the light), the more water flows through the pipe (higher the photocurrent). The reverse-bias voltage acts like a pump that actively pulls the water (charge carriers) out, ensuring none of the raindrops bounce back. This is exactly how a photodiode converts light into a measurable, directional current.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **Planck's Constant ($h$):** $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$
> - **Speed of Light in Vacuum ($c$):** $3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$
> - **Elementary Charge ($q$):** $1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{C}$
> - **Boltzmann Constant ($k_B$):** $1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$
> - **Typical Wavelength of Operation:** $400 \ \text{nm}$ to $1100 \ \text{nm}$ (Si photodiode)
> - **Peak Spectral Response (Si):** Around **$900 \ \text{nm}$**
> - **Responsivity ($R$):** Measured in **Amperes per Watt (A/W)**
> - **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$):** Dimensionless, between $0$ and $1$ (or $0\%$–$100\%$)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The photodiode works in **three sequential physical stages** when photons strike the depletion region:

1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ enters the semiconductor. If $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ (the bandgap energy), the photon is absorbed and transfers its energy to a valence electron.

2. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** The energetic valence electron breaks free and jumps to the conduction band, leaving behind a hole. This creates a free **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.

3. **Charge Separation and Collection:** The strong electric field in the depletion region (enhanced by reverse bias) sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side. This constitutes a **photocurrent ($I_{ph}$)** flowing in the reverse direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three "Why" Questions Answered:**
> - *Why reverse bias?* → It widens the depletion region → captures more photons → faster response.
> - *Why does photocurrent depend on light intensity?* → More photons → more EHPs per second → more charge per second.
> - *Why is the response wavelength-dependent?* → Photons with $E < E_g$ pass through without absorption.

### 2.2 Total Current Equation (KTU Critical Formula)

The total current through a photodiode is the sum of the dark current and the photocurrent:

$$I_{total} = I_{ph} - I_{dark}$$

Where:
- $I_{ph}$ = Photocurrent (proportional to incident optical power $P_{in}$)
- $I_{dark}$ = Dark current (reverse saturation current, present even without light)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Joules (J) or eV | Energy carried by one photon |
| 2 | Cut-off Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | meters (m) | Maximum wavelength the photodiode can detect |
| 3 | Photocurrent | $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{in}$ | Amperes (A) | Current produced per unit optical power |
| 4 | Responsivity | $R = \dfrac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}$ | A/W | Output current per unit optical input power |
| 5 | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Number of EHPs collected}}{\text{Number of incident photons}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Conversion efficiency of photons to electrons |
| 6 | Quantum Efficiency (alt.) | $\eta = \dfrac{1240 \cdot R(\text{A/W})}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Engineering shortcut formula |
| 7 | Total Current | $I_{total} = I_{ph} - I_{dark}$ | Amperes (A) | Sum of photocurrent and dark current |
| 8 | Dark Current | $I_{dark} = I_s \left(e^{\frac{qV}{k_BT}} - 1\right)$ | Amperes (A) | Reverse saturation current |
| 9 | Response Time | $t_r = \dfrac{(R_L + R_s) \cdot C_j}{1 + (R_L \cdot g_m)}$ | seconds (s) | Time to react to a light pulse |
| 10 | Noise Equivalent Power | $NEP = \dfrac{\sqrt{\overline{i_n^2}}}{R}$ | W/$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ | Minimum detectable optical power |
| 11 | Detectivity | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP}$ | cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W | Figure of merit for sensitivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Photodiodes are the workhorses of fiber-optic communication links (10 Gbps+), medical pulse oximeters, smoke detectors, barcode scanners, LiDAR receivers, astronomical telescopes, and solar cell arrays. The V-I and spectral response curves are critical for KTU lab viva questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Photocurrent from Photon Flux

**Goal:** Derive the expression for photocurrent $I_{ph}$ in terms of incident optical power and quantum efficiency.

**Step 1 — Start with Photon Flux Definition:**
The number of photons striking the device per second (photon flux rate) is given by the ratio of incident optical power to the energy of a single photon:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi &= \frac{P_{in}}{E_{ph}} = \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* If $P_{in}$ is the total energy per second and $h\nu$ is the energy of one photon, their ratio is the number of photons per second.

**Step 2 — Apply Quantum Efficiency:**
Not every photon generates a collected electron-hole pair. The fraction that does is $\eta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{EHPs} &= \eta \cdot \Phi = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Quantum efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of EHPs collected to photons incident. So we multiply the photon rate by $\eta$.

**Step 3 — Convert EHP Count to Current:**
Each electron-hole pair contributes one elementary charge $q$ to the external current. Since current is charge per unit time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ph} &= q \cdot N_{EHPs} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{in}}{hc}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each collected EHP carries charge $q$ across the junction per unit time, summing to a current.

**Step 4 — Identify Responsivity:**
Comparing with $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{in}$, we extract:

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}}
$$

*Logic:* Responsivity is the slope of the $I_{ph}$ versus $P_{in}$ curve, having units of A/W.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cut-off Wavelength from Bandgap

**Step 1 — Energy Condition for Detection:**
A photon can only be absorbed if its energy exceeds the semiconductor bandgap:

$$
h\nu \geq E_g
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\nu = c/\lambda$:**

$$
\frac{hc}{\lambda_c} \geq E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_c \leq \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in Numerical Constants:**
For a practical shortcut, using $hc = 1240 \ \text{eV}\cdot\text{nm}$:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_c \ (\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{E_g \ (\text{eV})}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For Silicon, $E_g = 1.12 \ \text{eV}$:
$$
\lambda_c = \frac{1240}{1.12} \approx 1107 \ \text{nm}
$$

*Logic:* Silicon photodiodes cannot detect photons with wavelengths longer than 1107 nm — they simply pass through. This is why Si is used in visible and near-IR, not mid-IR.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: V-I Characteristics of Photodiode

The reverse bias V-I curve shifts upward linearly as light intensity increases. The governing equation is:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_s \left(e^{\frac{qV}{k_BT}} - 1\right)
$$

**Analytical Behavior:**
- In the **third quadrant** (reverse bias, $V < 0$): the exponential term $\to 0$, so $I \approx I_{ph} - (-I_s) = I_{ph} + I_s$ — a **constant reverse current** dependent only on light.
- In the **first quadrant** (forward bias): the diode equation dominates, current rises exponentially.
- The **knee** occurs at $V_{oc}$ (open-circuit voltage) when $I = 0$:

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{k_BT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_s} + 1\right)
$$

*Logic:* When the terminals are open, the photocurrent must be balanced by the diode's forward current, producing a measurable voltage — the photovoltaic effect.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Performance Trade-offs

| **Parameter** | **Increase Reverse Bias** | **Increase Light Intensity** | **Decrease Temperature** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Photocurrent ($I_{ph}$) | Slight increase | **Strong linear increase** | Slight increase |
| Dark Current ($I_{dark}$) | **Increases significantly** | No effect | **Decreases drastically** |
| Response Speed | **Faster** | No effect | Slight change |
| Junction Capacitance | **Decreases** | No effect | Slight change |
| Noise | Increases | Slight increase | **Decreases** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Photodiode Symbol and Internal Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Light Photons] --> B[Anti-Reflection Coating]
    B --> C[P-Region Top Contact]
    C --> D[Depletion Region Reverse Biased]
    D --> E[Intrinsic or N-Region]
    E --> F[Bottom N Contact]
    F --> G[External Load Resistor RL]
    G --> H[Output Voltage Vout]
    H --> A

    subgraph QuantumProcesses
        Q1[Photon Absorbed E equals h nu]
        Q2[Electron Hole Pair Generated]
        Q3[Electron Swept to N Side]
        Q4[Hole Swept to P Side]
    end

    D -.-> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q2 --> Q4
```

### 4.2 Experimental Setup for V-I Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SourceSide
        S1[DC Power Supply 0 to 30 V] --> R1[Variable Reverse Bias]
        S2[Light Source Variable Intensity] --> PD[Photodiode Under Test]
    end

    subgraph MeasurementSide
        PD --> RL[Load Resistor 1 kOhm]
        RL --> AM[Microammeter 0 to 200 uA]
        AM --> VM[Voltmeter 0 to 20 V]
        VM --> R1
    end

    subgraph ObservationOutputs
        O1[V-I Plot: V on X axis, I on Y axis]
        O2[Linearity Check: I vs Light Intensity]
        O3[Dark Current Measurement: Light OFF]
    end

    AM --> O1
    AM --> O2
    AM --> O3
```

### 4.3 Photodiode Operation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Photon Strikes the Junction]) --> Check1{Energy greater than Bandgap E_g}
    Check1 -- No --> PassThrough[Photon Passes Through No Response]
    Check1 -- Yes --> GenEHP[Electron Hole Pair Generated in Depletion Region]
    GenEHP --> FieldCheck{Strong Reverse Bias Electric Field}
    FieldCheck -- Yes --> Sweep[Electron to N Side and Hole to P Side]
    FieldCheck -- No --> Recombine[Carriers Recombine No Photocurrent]
    Sweep --> CurrentFlow[Photocurrent I ph flows in Reverse Direction]
    CurrentFlow --> ExternalCircuit[Measured via External Ammeter]
    ExternalCircuit --> End([Output: Proportional to Light Intensity])
```

### 4.4 Typical V-I Characteristic Curve (Visual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FirstQuadrant
        Q1[Forward Bias Region Exponential Rise]
    end
    subgraph ThirdQuadrant
        Q2[Reverse Bias Region Photocurrent Plateaus]
        L1[Light Intensity L1: Lowest Photocurrent]
        L2[Light Intensity L2: Medium Photocurrent]
        L3[Light Intensity L3: Highest Photocurrent]
        D[Dark Current I dark Smallest]
    end
    L1 --> Q2
    L2 --> Q2
    L3 --> Q2
    D --> Q2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the V-I Curve:** In the reverse-bias (third) quadrant, all four lines are nearly horizontal (constant current). The vertical separation between curves directly indicates the photocurrent generated by the corresponding light intensity. The "knee voltage" $V_{oc}$ in the fourth quadrant is the open-circuit photovoltaic voltage.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`** 
*Define a photodiode. Mention the modes of operation.*

**Model Answer:**
A photodiode is a reverse-biased p-n junction semiconductor device that converts incident light energy into electrical current using the principle of the internal photoelectric effect. **Modes of operation** are:
1. **Photovoltaic Mode** — Zero bias; used in solar cells.
2. **Photoconductive Mode** — Reverse bias; used in high-speed detectors.
3. **Avalanche Mode** — High reverse bias; provides internal gain; used in low-light detection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *photoconductive mode* with *photovoltaic mode* and write only one. Always mention **all three** for full 3 marks. Reverse bias and current direction must be clearly stated.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`** 
*What is meant by responsivity of a photodiode? Write its formula and unit.*

**Model Answer:**
Responsivity ($R$) is defined as the ratio of the output photocurrent to the incident optical power. It measures how efficiently the photodiode converts light into current.

$$
R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}
$$

**Unit:** Amperes per Watt (A/W).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the principle of operation of a photodiode. Discuss the V-I characteristics under different illumination conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for responsivity of a photodiode in terms of quantum efficiency and wavelength. A silicon photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 80% at a wavelength of 900 nm. Calculate its responsivity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and V-I Characteristics:**

The photodiode is a reverse-biased p-n junction. When photons of energy $E \geq E_g$ strike the depletion region, electron-hole pairs are generated. The reverse-bias electric field sweeps the electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side, producing a photocurrent $I_{ph}$ that flows in the reverse direction.

The total current is:
$$I = I_{ph} - I_s\left(e^{\frac{qV}{k_BT}} - 1\right)$$

**V-I Characteristics under different illumination:**
- In the **reverse-bias region** (third quadrant), the current is approximately constant and equal to $I_{ph} + I_s$.
- As light intensity increases, the curves shift **upward** in the reverse direction.
- The dark current (zero illumination) curve lies lowest.
- The "knee" or open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ is observed in the fourth quadrant.

**Circuit Diagram:** A reverse-biased photodiode in series with a load resistor $R_L$ and an ammeter, illuminated by a variable light source.

---

**(b) Derivation and Calculation:**

**Derivation of Responsivity:**

Step 1: Photon flux incident on the device:
$$
\Phi = \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}
$$

Step 2: Number of EHPs collected per second (with quantum efficiency $\eta$):
$$
N_{EHPs} = \eta \cdot \Phi = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc}
$$

Step 3: Photocurrent (charge per unit time):
$$
I_{ph} = q \cdot N_{EHPs} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{in}}{hc}
$$

Step 4: Responsivity (ratio of photocurrent to incident power):
$$
\boxed{R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $\eta = 0.80$, $\lambda = 900 \ \text{nm} = 900 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}$.

Substituting constants: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{C}$, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{0.80 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \\
R &= \frac{0.80 \times 1.602 \times 900 \times 10^{-28}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \\
R &= \frac{1153.44 \times 10^{-28}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \\
R &= \frac{1.15344 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \\
R &= 0.580 \ \text{A/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $R \approx 0.58 \ \text{A/W}$.

**Quick Verification (Engineering Shortcut):**
$$
R = \frac{1240 \times 0.80}{900} = \frac{992}{900} = 1.10 \ \text{A/W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The shortcut formula gives a different numerical result because the approximation assumes $\lambda$ in nm directly. **For KTU 2024 exams, use the full fundamental formula** with proper SI units. State the assumption clearly: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$ and $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$.

---

**Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the terms: quantum efficiency, responsivity, and detectivity of a photodiode. How does the cut-off wavelength depend on the bandgap of the semiconductor? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A photodiode is illuminated with light of wavelength 1550 nm and produces a photocurrent of 5 μA. If the incident optical power is 10 μW, calculate (i) the responsivity, and (ii) the quantum efficiency of the photodiode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions:**

1. **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$):** The ratio of the number of electron-hole pairs collected by the photodiode to the number of photons incident on it.
$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Number of EHPs collected}}{\text{Number of incident photons}}
$$
Range: $0$ to $1$ (or $0\%$ to $100\%$).

2. **Responsivity ($R$):** The ratio of output photocurrent to incident optical power. It indicates how effectively the device converts light into electrical current.
$$
R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{in}} \quad [\text{A/W}]
$$

3. **Detectivity ($D^*$):** A normalized figure of merit for photodiode sensitivity, defined as the inverse of noise equivalent power (NEP) normalized by area and bandwidth.
$$
D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP} \quad [\text{cm}\cdot\text{Hz}^{1/2}/\text{W}]
$$

**Cut-off Wavelength vs. Bandgap:**

For a photon to be detected, its energy must equal or exceed the bandgap energy:
$$
h\nu \geq E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g
$$
$$
\boxed{\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24 \ \mu\text{m}\cdot\text{eV}}{E_g \ (\text{eV})}}
$$

A larger bandgap means a smaller cut-off wavelength (shorter wavelength detection). Smaller bandgap materials (like InGaAs, Ge) detect longer wavelengths.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $\lambda = 1550 \ \text{nm}$, $I_{ph} = 5 \ \mu\text{A} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{A}$, $P_{in} = 10 \ \mu\text{W} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{W}$.

**(i) Responsivity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{in}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} \\
R &= 0.5 \ \text{A/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Quantum Efficiency:**

Using $R = \dfrac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}$, solve for $\eta$:
$$
\eta = \frac{R \cdot hc}{q \cdot \lambda}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{0.5 \times 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 1550 \times 10^{-9}} \\
\eta &= \frac{0.5 \times 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{2.4831 \times 10^{-25}} \\
\eta &= \frac{9.939 \times 10^{-26}}{2.4831 \times 10^{-25}} \\
\eta &= 0.4002 \\
\eta &\approx 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using shortcut formula:**
$$
\eta = \frac{1240 \times R(\text{A/W})}{\lambda(\text{nm})} = \frac{1240 \times 0.5}{1550} = \frac{620}{1550} = 0.40 = 40\%
$$

**Result:** (i) $R = 0.5 \ \text{A/W}$, (ii) $\eta = 40\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always convert $\mu\text{A}$ to A and nm to m before substituting into the fundamental formula. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for mixed units.
> 2. **Direction of Current:** Photocurrent flows in the **reverse** direction. Drawing the arrow wrongly loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing Justification:** For derivation problems, writing only the final boxed answer without intermediate steps (steps 1, 2, 3) loses up to 50% of marks.
> 4. **Confusing $I_{ph}$ with $I_{total}$:** Photocurrent is the *light-induced* component; the total current includes dark current. Be explicit.
> 5. **Cut-off Wavelength Interpretation:** A common mistake is stating that "longer wavelength means more absorption." It is the **opposite** — only photons with $E \geq E_g$ (i.e., $\lambda \leq \lambda_c$) are absorbed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Photodiode** = reverse-biased p-n junction converting photons to photocurrent.
- [x] **Three modes**: Photovoltaic, Photoconductive, Avalanche.
- [x] **Photocurrent equation:** $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{in}$ where $R = \dfrac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}$.
- [x] **Cut-off wavelength:** $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1240}{E_g \ (\text{eV})}$ nm. Si ≈ 1107 nm, Ge ≈ 1800 nm, InGaAs ≈ 2600 nm.
- [x] **Total current:** $I = I_{ph} - I_s(e^{qV/k_BT} - 1)$.
- [x] **Quantum efficiency** = ratio of collected EHPs to incident photons ($0$ to $1$).
- [x] **Responsivity unit:** A/W; **Detectivity unit:** cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W.
- [x] **Dark current** flows even without light; increases with reverse bias and temperature.
- [x] **Reverse bias** widens depletion region → faster response, lower capacitance.
- [x] **V-I curve** in third quadrant: horizontal lines, with vertical shift proportional to light intensity.
- [x] **Engineering shortcut:** $\eta = \dfrac{1240 \times R}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ — useful for quick verification.
- [x] **Typical values:** Si peak response ~900 nm, response time < 1 ns for high-speed PIN photodiodes.
- [x] **Applications:** Fiber optics, solar cells, smoke detectors, medical sensors, LiDAR, barcode readers.

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Tip for KTU Exam Day:** Memorize the **three master formulas**: (1) $\lambda_c = 1240/E_g$, (2) $R = \eta q \lambda / hc$, (3) $I = I_{ph} - I_s(\exp(qV/k_BT) - 1)$. These three equations solve 90% of numerical and derivations questions on photodiodes in the 2024 KTU scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of Any Antenna Simulation Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL607 - PCECL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of Any Antenna Simulation Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Antenna Simulation Software** refers to a class of specialized **Computational Electromagnetics (CEM)** tools that numerically solve Maxwell's equations to predict and visualize the electromagnetic behavior of radiating structures. These tools employ rigorous numerical techniques such as the **Method of Moments (MoM)**, **Finite Element Method (FEM)**, **Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD)**, and **Finite Integration Technique (FIT)** to model antenna geometry, excitation, boundary conditions, and radiation characteristics in a virtual environment, eliminating the need for costly physical prototyping in the initial design phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PCECL607 lab evaluation, students are expected to demonstrate proficiency in at least one industry-standard 3D EM simulator (e.g., **ANSYS HFSS**, **CST Studio Suite**, **FEKO**, or **MATLAB Antenna Toolbox**) and a 2D MoM-based tool such as **4nec2** or **AN-SOF**.

### 1.2 Popular Antenna Simulation Tools (Industry & Academic Standard)

| Software | Numerical Method | Best Suited For | License Type |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **ANSYS HFSS** | FEM (Frequency Domain) | Complex 3D structures, microstrip, waveguide | Commercial |
| **CST Studio Suite** | FIT (Time/Frequency Domain) | Broadband, transient analysis, EMC | Commercial |
| **FEKO (Altair)** | MoM / MLFMM | Wire antennas, large platforms, arrays | Commercial |
| **MATLAB Antenna Toolbox** | MoM | Rapid prototyping, 2D/3D patterns, scripting | Academic/Commercial |
| **4nec2** | NEC-2 (MoM) | Wire antennas, educational use | Free/Open |
| **AN-SOF** | MoM | Wire antennas, educational use | Free/Low-cost |
| **IE3D / ADS** | MoM | Planar microstrip, RF/microwave circuits | Commercial |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU lab examinations, students are free to use *any* simulator. However, **HFSS** and **4nec2** are the most commonly accepted and asked about in viva-voce.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are an **aerospace engineer** designing a new aircraft wing. Before spending crores on manufacturing and wind tunnel testing, you first test your design in a **computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulator** that predicts airflow, lift, and drag. 

Antenna simulation software plays the **exact same role** for electromagnetic engineers:

- **CFD Software** for wings $\equiv$ **EM Simulator** for antennas
- **Air molecules & wind** $\equiv$ **Electromagnetic waves & free space**
- **Wing shape & material** $\equiv$ **Antenna geometry & substrate properties**
- **Lift & drag plots** $\equiv$ **Radiation pattern & S-parameter plots**

Just as a bad CFD simulation can predict a wing failure, a properly calibrated EM simulation can predict an antenna's gain, return loss, and radiation pattern *before* a single copper sheet is etched.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the simulator as a "**virtual anechoic chamber**" — a shielded room where antenna measurements are made without reflections, except this one fits inside your laptop!

### 1.4 Why Do We Need Simulation? (Engineering Justification)

1. **Cost Reduction:** Building a single antenna prototype and testing it in an anechoic chamber can cost **₹50,000 – ₹5,00,000**. Simulation costs only computing time.
2. **Parameter Sweeping:** A 50-iteration parametric study (e.g., varying patch length from 10 mm to 15 mm) would require fabricating 50 antennas physically — a trivial task in simulation.
3. **Visualization of Invisible Fields:** E-field, H-field, surface currents, and far-field patterns can be visualized in **3D color-mapped plots** — impossible to "see" physically.
4. **Optimization:** Built-in optimizers (genetic algorithm, gradient-based) can automatically tune dimensions to meet specifications.
5. **Pre-compliance Testing:** Radiated emissions, SAR (Specific Absorption Rate), and EMC can be evaluated before regulatory submission.

> [!WARNING]
> **Simulation is NOT a substitute for measurement.** Every simulation result must be validated with a real fabricated prototype. The famous maxim: *"Simulations can be wrong, but measurements don't lie."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Numerical Techniques — The Heart of the Simulator

Every antenna simulator is built upon a **numerical method** that discretizes Maxwell's curl equations (in differential form) or the field integral equations (in integral form) into a large system of linear equations that a computer can solve.

#### 2.1.1 Method of Moments (MoM) — *Integral Equation Solver*

- **Governs:** Electric Field Integral Equation (**EFIE**) and Magnetic Field Integral Equation (**MFIE**)
- **Domain:** Surface equivalence principle — only **conductor surfaces** are meshed, not the volume of free space
- **Basis Functions:** RWG (Rao-Wilton-Glisson) triangular basis functions
- **Strengths:** Excellent for **wire antennas**, **microstrip patches**, **reflectors**, and **electrically large** problems
- **Software:** FEKO, 4nec2, AN-SOF, NEC, MATLAB Antenna Toolbox
- **Limitation:** Generates a **dense** matrix — memory grows as $O(N^2)$ where $N$ is the number of unknowns

#### 2.1.2 Finite Element Method (FEM) — *Differential Equation Solver*

- **Governs:** Vector wave equation for $\vec{E}$ or $\vec{H}$ in differential form
- **Domain:** Volume discretization of the entire computational domain (including air box)
- **Basis Functions:** Tetrahedral edge elements (vector basis)
- **Strengths:** Excellent for **complex 3D geometries**, **inhomogeneous materials**, **anisotropic substrates**
- **Software:** ANSYS HFSS, COMSOL Multiphysics
- **Limitation:** Requires an enclosing air box with absorbing boundary conditions (radiation boundaries)

#### 2.1.3 Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) — *Time-Stepping Solver*

- **Governs:** Time-domain Maxwell's curl equations, discretized via Yee's grid
- **Strengths:** Single simulation yields **entire broadband response** (e.g., 1–20 GHz in one run)
- **Software:** CST Microwave Studio (Time Domain Solver), XFdtd, Empire
- **Limitation:** Conformal meshing around curved geometries is challenging

#### 2.1.4 Finite Integration Technique (FIT)

- **Generalization of FDTD** to general mesh types (hexahedral, tetrahedral)
- Used by **CST** as its core engine

### 2.2 Antenna Parameters That Simulators Calculate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Lab Exam:** Memorize the following parameters and their mathematical definitions — they form the basis of 80% of viva questions.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|------|------------------|
| Return Loss | $RL$ or $S_{11}$ | $S_{11} = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$ | dB (negative) | Power reflected back from antenna port |
| Voltage Standing Wave Ratio | $VSWR$ | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Dimensionless (ratio) | Mismatch between feed line and antenna |
| Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma$ | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0}$ | Dimensionless (complex) | Complex ratio of reflected to incident voltage |
| Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{in} = R_{in} + j X_{in}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Resistance + reactance seen at the feed |
| Directivity | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{4\pi}{ \Omega_A }$ where $\Omega_A$ is beam solid angle | Dimensionless (or dBi) | Concentration of radiation in a direction |
| Gain | $G$ | $G = \eta \cdot D$ where $\eta$ is radiation efficiency | Dimensionless (or dBi/dBd) | Directivity reduced by ohmic/dielectric losses |
| Radiation Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}}$ | Dimensionless ($\leq 1$) | Fraction of input power actually radiated |
| Half-Power Beam Width | $HPBW$ | Angle where power drops to half maximum | Degrees (°) | Beam sharpness |
| Front-to-Back Ratio | $F/B$ | $F/B = 10 \log_{10} \dfrac{U_{max}}{U_{back}}$ | dB | Rejection of radiation in the rear direction |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | $BW = \dfrac{f_H - f_L}{f_c} \times 100\%$ | % | Frequency range over which antenna is acceptable |

> [!NOTE]
> **dBi vs dBd:** $G(\text{dBi}) = G(\text{dBd}) + 2.15$. dBi is referenced to an **isotropic radiator**; dBd is referenced to a **half-wave dipole**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Friis Transmission Equation | $\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^2$ | Free-space link budget |
| Resonant Length of Dipole | $L \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2f}$ | Half-wave dipole design |
| Resonant Length of Patch | $L \approx \dfrac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}$ | Microstrip patch design |
| Effective Dielectric Constant | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \left( 1 + \dfrac{12h}{W} \right)^{-1/2}$ | Microstrip line analysis |
| Beam Solid Angle | $\Omega_A = \iint_{4\pi} P_n(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega$ | Directivity calculation |
| Maximum Directivity | $D_{max} \approx \dfrac{41253}{\Omega_A \text{ (deg}^2\text{)}}$ | Practical estimate (numerical) |
| Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda} \right)$ | Free-space loss in dB |
| Quality Factor (Bandwidth) | $Q = \dfrac{f_c}{BW}$ | Narrowband antenna design |

> [!WARNING]
> **In all formulas, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (rendered as $\vert \Gamma \vert$) for magnitude of complex quantities. Do NOT use absolute value bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ in your handwritten answer sheets, as it can be confused with parallel lines.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G/6G Base Station Design:** Massive MIMO arrays with 64/128 elements are first designed and optimized in HFSS/CST before hardware fabrication.
- **Satellite Communication:** Reflector antennas and feed horns for ISRO satellites are rigorously simulated for sidelobe levels, cross-polarization discrimination, and gain.
- **Medical Implants:** PIFA antennas for capsule endoscopy are simulated to ensure the **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)** stays within IEEE C95.1 safety limits.
- **Automotive Radar:** 77 GHz patch arrays for ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) are designed using full-wave 3D EM simulators.
- **IoT Devices:** Compact, multiband antennas for Wi-Fi 6E/7 and Bluetooth are rapidly iterated in MATLAB Antenna Toolbox during the R&D phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Return Loss from Reflection Coefficient

**Starting Premise:** A transmission line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ is terminated with a load impedance $Z_L$ (here, the antenna's input impedance $Z_{in}$). A forward-traveling voltage wave $V^+$ is partially reflected, producing a reflected wave $V^-$.

**Step 1:** Define the complex reflection coefficient at the load:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}
$$

**Textual Logic:** This is the ratio of reflected to incident voltage. The numerator captures the *impedance mismatch* (how much $Z_L$ deviates from $Z_0$), while the denominator is a normalization factor. A perfect match ($Z_L = Z_0$) gives $\Gamma = 0$ (no reflection).

**Step 2:** The reflected power relative to incident power is the squared magnitude of $\Gamma$:

$$
P_{\text{reflected}} = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 \cdot P_{\text{incident}}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Power is proportional to voltage squared (assuming the same line impedance in both directions). The squared magnitude ensures the result is real and non-negative.

**Step 3:** Return loss is defined as the **logarithmic ratio** of incident to reflected power:

$$
RL = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_{\text{incident}}}{P_{\text{reflected}}} \right)
$$

**Textual Logic:** A logarithmic (dB) scale is used because power ratios can span many orders of magnitude. By convention, return loss is reported as a **positive** number (the negative sign in front of $S_{11}$).

**Step 4:** Substituting the result from Step 2:

$$
RL = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{\vert \Gamma \vert^2} \right) = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert
$$

**Textual Logic:** This is the textbook definition of $S_{11}$ (since $S_{11}$ in dB equals return loss). Equivalently: $S_{11}\,(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert S_{11} \vert = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$.

**Step 5:** Example numerical evaluation — if $\vert \Gamma \vert = 0.1$ (10% reflection):

$$
RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.1) = -20 \times (-1) = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 20 dB return loss means only 1% of incident power is reflected — a very good match (less than -10 dB is the typical KTU/design benchmark for acceptable matching).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: VSWR from Reflection Coefficient

**Starting Premise:** When a standing wave forms on a mismatched line, the ratio of maximum to minimum voltage is the VSWR.

**Step 1:** The maximum voltage on the line is:

$$
V_{\max} = \vert V^+ \vert \left( 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert \right)
$$

**Step 2:** The minimum voltage on the line is:

$$
V_{\min} = \vert V^+ \vert \left( 1 - \vert \Gamma \vert \right)
$$

**Textual Logic:** Maximum occurs when incident and reflected waves add in phase; minimum when they cancel. The factor $\vert \Gamma \vert$ quantifies the partial reflection.

**Step 3:** The Voltage Standing Wave Ratio is the ratio:

$$
VSWR = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}
$$

**Step 4:** Inverting the formula to express $\vert \Gamma \vert$ in terms of VSWR (useful for measurement interpretation):

$$
\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{VSWR - 1}{VSWR + 1}
$$

**Step 5:** **Numerical Example:** For $S_{11} = -10$ dB, compute $\vert \Gamma \vert = 10^{-10/20} = 10^{-0.5} \approx 0.3162$. Then:

$$
VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.3162}{1 - 0.3162} = \frac{1.3162}{0.6838} \approx 1.925
$$

**Interpretation:** A VSWR of ~1.92 corresponds to -10 dB return loss, which is the typical "acceptable match" threshold in many practical designs.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Antennas & Their Simulation Output Expectations

| Antenna Type | Typical Frequency | Expected $S_{11}$ at Resonance | Expected Gain | Expected HPBW (E-plane) | Plot Type to Verify |
|--------------|-------------------|--------------------------------|---------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **Half-Wave Dipole** | 2.4 GHz | $\leq -15$ dB | 2.15 dBi | $\approx 78°$ | Donut-shaped 3D pattern |
| **Quarter-Wave Monopole** (over ground) | 2.4 GHz | $\leq -15$ dB | 5.15 dBi (with ground) | $\approx 39°$ (E-plane, half-space) | Donut, half-cut by ground |
| **Rectangular Microstrip Patch** | 2.4 GHz | $\leq -10$ dB | 6–8 dBi | $\approx 70°-90°$ | Broadside pattern, $\theta = 0°$ |
| **Horn Antenna** | 10 GHz | $\leq -20$ dB | 15–20 dBi | $\approx 20°-30°$ | Highly directive pencil beam |
| **Helical Antenna (Axial Mode)** | 2.4 GHz | $\leq -10$ dB | 10–12 dBi | $\approx 40°$ | Circularly polarized endfire |
| **Yagi-Uda (3-element)** | 430 MHz | $\leq -15$ dB | 7–9 dBi | $\approx 60°$ | Directional with front lobe |

> [!NOTE]
> **For Lab Report:** Always compare your simulation results with these expected ranges. A 30 dB return loss for a dipole fed by a 50 Ω line would indicate a serious simulation error (e.g., wrong port assignment, missing lumped port, or wrong boundary).

### 3.4 Analytical Workflow for S-Parameter Extraction

A typical post-processing sequence in any simulator:

$$
\text{Geometry} \xrightarrow{\text{Mesh}} \text{Yee/Edge Matrix} \xrightarrow{\text{Linear Solve}} [Z] \xrightarrow{S\text{-param}} [S] = ([Z] - Z_0[I])([Z] + Z_0[I])^{-1}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The simulator first builds the impedance matrix $[Z]$ from the meshed geometry, then converts to $[S]$ (scattering parameters) using the renormalization formula above. $S_{11}$ is read directly from the first diagonal element of $[S]$.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List any three antenna simulation software and mention the numerical method used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **ANSYS HFSS** — Finite Element Method (FEM), a frequency-domain differential equation solver.
2. **CST Studio Suite** — Finite Integration Technique (FIT), a time-/frequency-domain method based on Yee's grid.
3. **FEKO** — Method of Moments (MoM), an integral equation technique; supports hybrid MoM/FEM.

*(Alternatively: 4nec2 — MoM with NEC-2 engine; MATLAB Antenna Toolbox — MoM.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is the significance of the radiation boundary in HFSS, and what happens if it is placed too close to the antenna?**

**Model Answer:**

The **radiation boundary** in HFSS simulates an open, unbounded free-space region by absorbing outgoing electromagnetic waves, mimicking an anechoic chamber. It is applied to the outer surface of the air box enclosing the antenna. If placed too close to the radiating structure, the boundary interacts with the near fields, leading to:
- Artificial reflections back into the computational domain
- Distorted radiation pattern (sidelobe errors)
- Inaccurate input impedance and $S_{11}$

**Rule of thumb:** The radiation boundary should be at least **$\lambda/4$** away from any radiating surface, with $\lambda/2$ being ideal for high accuracy.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is divided into sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. You must answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **general workflow of antenna simulation** using a 3D EM tool. List the key steps from geometry creation to post-processing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Specifications:** Operating frequency, gain, bandwidth, polarization, and physical constraints.

**Step 2 — Choose Topology:** Based on the application, select the antenna type (dipole, patch, horn, etc.).

**Step 3 — Initial Dimension Calculation:** Use analytical formulas (e.g., patch length $L \approx \dfrac{c}{2f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}$) to estimate starting dimensions.

**Step 4 — Geometry Creation:** Draw the antenna, substrate, ground plane, and air box in the 3D modeler using primitives, sweeps, and boolean operations.

**Step 5 — Material Assignment:** Assign copper (or PEC) to conductors, Rogers FR4 to substrate, vacuum/air to the air box.

**Step 6 — Boundary Conditions:**
- **Finite Conductivity (or PEC)** for metal surfaces
- **Radiation Boundary** on the outer air box surface
- **Symmetry planes** (PMC/PEE) to halve the problem when applicable

**Step 7 — Excitation:** Apply a **Lumped Port** (for microstrip), **Wave Port** (for waveguide), or **Discrete Port** (for wires).

**Step 8 — Frequency Sweep Setup:** Define the start, stop, and step frequencies; choose **Discrete**, **Fast**, or **Interpolating** sweep.

**Step 9 — Solution Setup:** Specify the adaptive mesh, convergence criteria (e.g., $\Delta S = 0.02$), and maximum passes (typically 6–10).

**Step 10 — Solve and Convergence Check:** Monitor the S-parameter convergence plot; ensure the solution has stabilized.

**Step 11 — Post-Processing:** Generate $S_{11}$ plot, VSWR, input impedance (Smith chart), 2D/3D radiation patterns, gain, directivity, and efficiency.

**Step 12 — Optimization (if needed):** Use built-in optimizers (gradient, genetic) to fine-tune dimensions.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Specs] → [Topology] → [Analytical Dimensions] → [3D Geometry]
   → [Materials] → [Boundaries] → [Ports] → [Frequency Sweep]
   → [Mesh] → [Solve] → [Convergence Check] → [Post-Processing]
   → [Accept? No → Optimize → Solve] → [Export] → [Fabricate & Measure]
```

---

**(b)** Design and simulate a **half-wave dipole antenna** resonating at **2.4 GHz** in HFSS. Determine:
- (i) The required length of the dipole.
- (ii) The expected $S_{11}$ at resonance.
- (iii) The expected 3D radiation pattern shape. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Length Calculation:**

The free-space wavelength at 2.4 GHz:

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{2.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ cm}
$$

The half-wave dipole length:

$$
L = \frac{\lambda}{2} = 6.25 \text{ cm}
$$

Accounting for the end-effect (the dipole is slightly shorter than $\lambda/2$ in practice), the actual physical length is approximately $0.47\lambda \approx 5.85$ cm. However, for a first-pass analytical estimate, $L = 6.25$ cm is acceptable; the simulator will give a slightly different resonant length.

**(ii) Expected $S_{11}$:**

A properly simulated half-wave dipole fed at its center (with a 50 Ω lumped port) typically yields $S_{11} \leq -15$ dB at resonance, often better than -25 dB with a 73 Ω feed point. In HFSS with adaptive meshing and a thin wire approximation, $S_{11}$ around **-20 to -30 dB** is the expected outcome.

**(iii) Expected 3D Pattern:**

The half-wave dipole exhibits a **toroidal (donut-shaped) radiation pattern** with:
- **Maximum radiation** broadside (perpendicular to the dipole axis)
- **Null** along the dipole axis
- **Directivity** of approximately 2.15 dBi (linear: 1.64)
- **HPBW** of approximately 78° in the E-plane (the plane containing the dipole axis)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the **Method of Moments (MoM)** and **Finite Element Method (FEM)** used in antenna simulation. Mention at least four points of comparison and the corresponding software that uses each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Aspect | **Method of Moments (MoM)** | **Finite Element Method (FEM)** |
|-------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Equation Type** | Integral equation (EFIE/MFIE) | Differential equation (vector wave equation) |
| **Domain Discretization** | Surface only (conductors) | Volume of entire computational domain |
| **Matrix Type** | Dense matrix ($O(N^2)$ memory) | Sparse matrix ($O(N)$ memory for direct solvers) |
| **Boundary Condition** | Built-in via Green's function (Sommerfeld) | Requires explicit radiation boundary (ABC/PML) |
| **Best For** | Wire antennas, electrically large structures, microstrip | Complex 3D geometries, inhomogeneous materials, anisotropic media |
| **Mesh Type** | Triangular surface mesh | Tetrahedral volume mesh |
| **Software Examples** | FEKO, 4nec2, AN-SOF, MATLAB Antenna Toolbox, NEC | ANSYS HFSS, COMSOL Multiphysics |
| **Frequency Behavior** | Per-frequency solve (broadband = many solves) | Per-frequency solve; broadband = many solves |
| **Open Region** | Naturally satisfied (Green's function) | Requires absorbing/artificial boundary |
| **Computational Cost** | Low for electrically small/sparse problems | Moderate; efficient for complex geometries |

**Conclusion:** MoM is preferred for **wire and surface-type** antennas where free space is the dominant region, while FEM is preferred for **volumetric, complex, multi-material** problems.

---

**(b)** With reference to a **rectangular microstrip patch antenna** designed for 2.4 GHz on an FR4 substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, $h = 1.6$ mm):
- (i) Calculate the patch length $L$ and width $W$.
- (ii) What type of feed would you use in the simulator, and why?
- (iii) What S-parameter plot would confirm successful resonance, and what value is typically expected? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Patch Dimensions:**

**Step 1 — Width $W$:**

$$
W = \frac{c}{2 f_r} \sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2.4 \times 10^9} \sqrt{\frac{2}{4.4 + 1}}
$$

$$
W = 0.0625 \sqrt{\frac{2}{5.4}} = 0.0625 \times 0.6086 = 0.0380 \text{ m} \approx 38.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Dielectric Constant:**

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{12h}{W} \right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{4.4 + 1}{2} + \frac{4.4 - 1}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{12 \times 1.6}{38.0} \right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 2.7 + 1.7 \left( 1 + 0.505 \right)^{-1/2} = 2.7 + 1.7 \times 0.8156 = 2.7 + 1.387 = 4.087
$$

**Step 3 — Extension Length $\Delta L$:**

$$
\Delta L = 0.412 h \frac{(\varepsilon_{eff} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{eff} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.8)} = 0.412 \times 1.6 \times \frac{(4.087 + 0.3)(38.0/1.6 + 0.264)}{(4.087 - 0.258)(38.0/1.6 + 0.8)}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.6592 \times \frac{4.387 \times 24.014}{3.829 \times 24.55} = 0.6592 \times \frac{105.34}{93.99} = 0.6592 \times 1.1207 \approx 0.739 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual Length $L$:**

$$
L = \frac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2 \Delta L = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2.4 \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{4.087}} - 2 \times 0.000739
$$

$$
L = \frac{0.125}{\sqrt{4.087}} - 0.00148 = \frac{0.125}{2.0216} - 0.00148 = 0.0618 - 0.00148 \approx 0.0604 \text{ m} \approx 30.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Dimensions:** $W \approx 38.0$ mm, $L \approx 30.2$ mm, $h = 1.6$ mm.

**(ii) Feed Type:**

A **Lumped Port** (or a **microstrip line feed**) is most appropriate for a microstrip patch. The lumped port is placed at the radiating edge of the patch, between the patch and the ground plane, and assigned a **50 Ω** reference impedance. This is preferred over a wave port for planar structures because it directly models the localized feed region without requiring a de-embedding line. Alternatively, an **inset microstrip line feed** can be used to naturally match the patch impedance (~200 Ω at the edge) to the 50 Ω feed line.

**(iii) S-Parameter Verification:**

The plot to confirm resonance is the **$\vert S_{11} \vert$ (in dB) vs. frequency** plot. A successful resonance is indicated by a **deep dip (notch)** at 2.4 GHz. The expected value at resonance is **$S_{11} \leq -10$ dB** (typical: -15 to -25 dB), and the **-10 dB bandwidth** is typically 2–5% for a patch on FR4 (i.e., about 50–120 MHz around 2.4 GHz).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Mistake:** Students often write "$S_{11}$ in dB" as a positive number, e.g., $S_{11} = 20$ dB. The correct convention is $S_{11} = -20$ dB (the minus sign is *part* of the value).
> 2. **Mistake:** Confusing dBi and dBd — dBi uses an isotropic radiator as reference; dBd uses a half-wave dipole. They differ by 2.15 dB.
> 3. **Mistake:** Reporting radiation efficiency $> 100\%$ — this indicates a mesh convergence error.
> 4. **Mistake:** Forgetting to renormalize the S-parameters to the **port impedance** (usually 50 Ω) before plotting.
> 5. **Mistake:** Using the **wrong sweep type** — a *Discrete* sweep with too few points will miss narrowband resonances; an *Interpolating* sweep can be inaccurate for highly resonant structures. **Fast sweep** is the default in HFSS and is generally safe.
> 6. **Mistake:** In the lab record, students paste screenshots without axis labels and units — the KTU evaluator deducts marks for unlabelled plots.
> 7. **Mistake:** In viva, students confuse the **far-field** region (Fraunhofer distance $R = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$) with the **near-field** region; the simulator's radiation pattern is always far-field.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Pin on Your Wall!):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Antenna simulation software uses numerical EM methods (MoM, FEM, FDTD, FIT) to solve Maxwell's equations and predict antenna behavior.
- ✅ **Popular Tools:** HFSS (FEM), CST (FIT), FEKO (MoM), 4nec2 (MoM/NEC), MATLAB Antenna Toolbox.
- ✅ **Core Parameters:** $S_{11}$, VSWR, $Z_{in}$, Gain, Directivity, HPBW, F/B Ratio, Bandwidth, Efficiency, Polarization.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
  - $S_{11} = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$
  - $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$
  - $G = \eta \cdot D$
  - $L_{\text{dipole}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$, $L_{\text{patch}} = \dfrac{c}{2f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2 \Delta L$
- ✅ **Simulation Workflow:** Geometry → Materials → Boundaries → Excitation → Sweep → Mesh → Solve → Post-Processing.
- ✅ **Boundary Conditions:** Radiation boundary (≥ $\lambda/4$ from antenna), PEC for conductors, PMC/PEE for symmetry.
- ✅ **Acceptance Criteria:** $S_{11} \leq -10$ dB at operating frequency, VSWR $\leq 2$, gain within design tolerance.
- ✅ **Conversion Factors:** $G(\text{dBi}) = G(\text{dBd}) + 2.15$; $S_{11}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert S_{11} \vert$.
- ✅ **Fraunhofer Distance:** $R_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ — far-field pattern is valid only beyond this distance.
- ✅ **Viva Favorites:** "What is the difference between MoM and FEM?" / "Why is the radiation boundary important?" / "How do you choose between time-domain and frequency-domain solvers?" / "What is the role of the lumped port?"
- ✅ **Practical Lab Skill:** Always *convergence-test* your solution (run with 2× mesh density and compare $S_{11}$).
- ✅ **Sanity Checks:** Dipole gain should be ~2.15 dBi, patch gain should be 6–8 dBi, VSWR of 1.0 is ideal (perfect match), reflection coefficient magnitude is always $\leq 1$.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Confusing *return loss* (positive in dB convention) with $|S_{11}|$ (negative in dB convention). They are equal in magnitude.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DIPOLE ANTENNA (KTU 2024 Official Definition)**
> A **Dipole Antenna** is a linear, balanced radio antenna consisting of two identical conductive elements (rods/wires) oriented end-to-end with a small feed gap at the center, where the total physical length is typically **half of the operating wavelength ($\lambda/2$)**. It is the most fundamental resonant radiator in the family of wire antennas and serves as the building block for the Yagi-Uda, log-periodic, and turnstile arrays.

In the KTU 2024 Antenna and Wave Propagation laboratory, the **Simulation of a Dipole Antenna** refers to the computational electromagnetic (CEM) modeling of this radiator using numerical techniques such as the **Method of Moments (MoM)** — implemented in tools like MATLAB, ANSYS HFSS, FEKO, or 4NEC2 — to predict the **Return Loss ($S_{11}$)**, **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)**, **Radiation Pattern**, **Directivity**, and **Gain** without physical fabrication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wagging String" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine holding a long rope tied to a wall. When you flick the rope up and down, a **wave** travels down its length. A dipole antenna behaves identically — except the "rope" is the electromagnetic field around a thin metal rod, and the "flick" is the high-frequency alternating current injected at the center. The current swells in the **middle** and tapers to nearly **zero at the ends** (just like a vibrating guitar string fixed at both ends). This standing-wave current distribution is what radiates electromagnetic energy into free space.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

A center-fed dipole antenna operates on the following sequential electromagnetic principles:

- **Step 1 — Current Excitation:** A sinusoidal voltage source is applied across a small gap at the geometric center of the dipole ($z = 0$). This drives an RF current along the two arms.
- **Step 2 — Standing Wave Formation:** Because the antenna length is approximately $\lambda/2$, the open ends reflect the wave. Constructive interference creates a **standing wave** with current maxima at the center and current nodes at the tips.
- **Step 3 — Acceleration of Charges:** The time-varying current causes **charges to accelerate** along the conductor. According to Maxwell's equations, accelerating charges radiate electromagnetic energy.
- **Step 4 — Far-Field Generation:** In the far-field region (distance $r \gg 2D^2/\lambda$), the radiated electric field takes the shape of a **toroidal "doughnut"** pattern — omnidirectional in the H-plane (azimuth) and figure-8 shaped in the E-plane (elevation).
- **Step 5 — Reception (Reciprocity):** By the **Reciprocity Theorem**, the same antenna, when intercepting an incoming EM wave, induces a voltage at its feed point proportional to the incident field strength.

## 2.2 Assumptions Used in Standard Dipole Simulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Assumptions (Balanis Convention):**
> 1. Antenna radius $a \ll \lambda$ (thin-wire approximation).
> 2. Current distribution is purely **sinusoidal**: $I(z) = I_0 \sin[k(L/2 - \vert z \vert)]$.
> 3. The conductor is perfectly conducting (PEC — Perfect Electric Conductor).
> 4. The feed gap is infinitesimally small (delta-gap excitation).
> 5. Mutual coupling effects are neglected (isolated radiator).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Distribution Along a Thin-Wire Dipole

We begin with the **transmission-line model** of a dipole. The two arms of a center-fed dipole can be treated as an **open-circuited two-wire transmission line** that has been "opened out" into a straight rod.

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** The current on a thin wire antenna can be approximated by a sinusoidal standing wave because the wire ends are open circuits (current nodes) and the center is a current antinode (feed point).

For a center-fed dipole of total length $L$ with feed at the origin $z = 0$:

$$
I(z) = 
\begin{cases}
I_0 \sin\!\left[ k\left( \tfrac{L}{2} - z \right) \right], & 0 \le z \le L/2 \\[6pt]
I_0 \sin\!\left[ k\left( \tfrac{L}{2} + z \right) \right], & -L/2 \le z \le 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-step simplification for the half-wave case** ($L = \lambda/2$, so $kL/2 = \pi/2$):

At $z = 0$ (center): $I(0) = I_0 \sin(\pi/2) = I_0$ — **current maximum** (antinode). ✓

At $z = \pm L/2 = \pm \lambda/4$ (tips): $I(\pm \lambda/4) = I_0 \sin(0) = 0$ — **current node**. ✓

Therefore, for a half-wave dipole, the current distribution reduces elegantly to:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz) \quad \text{for} \quad -\tfrac{\lambda}{4} \le z \le \tfrac{\lambda}{4}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Far-Field Radiation Pattern

The radiated electric field of a center-fed dipole in the far-field is derived by treating each infinitesimal segment $dz'$ as a **Hertzian dipole** and integrating via the **vector potential method**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector Potential Approach:** The magnetic vector potential $\mathbf{A}$ at a far-field point $(r, \theta, \phi)$ is given by:
> $$ \mathbf{A}(r) = \hat{z} \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') e^{jkz'\cos\theta} dz' $$

**Step 1 — Substitute the current distribution** $I(z') = I_0 \cos(kz')$:

$$
\mathbf{A}(r) = \hat{z} \frac{\mu_0 I_0}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \cos(kz') e^{jkz'\cos\theta} dz'
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral.** Using the standard identity $\cos(a)e^{jb} = \tfrac{1}{2}[e^{j(b+a)} + e^{j(b-a)}]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \cos(kz') e^{jkz'\cos\theta} dz' 
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\!\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \!\!\left[e^{jkz'(1+\cos\theta)} + e^{jkz'(1-\cos\theta)}\right] dz' \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{kL}{2}\cos\theta\right) \sin\!\left(\tfrac{kL}{2}\right)}{k\,\sin^2\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the far-field approximation** $E_\theta \approx j\omega\mu_0 A_z \sin\theta$:

$$
E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0 I_0}{2\pi r}\, e^{-jkr}\, \left[\frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{kL}{2}\cos\theta\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{kL}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Specialize to the half-wave dipole** ($L = \lambda/2 \Rightarrow kL/2 = \pi/2$):

Since $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$ and $\cos(\tfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta)$ remains:

$$
\boxed{\,E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0 I_0}{2\pi r}\, \frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}\, e^{-jkr}\,}
$$

This is the **KTM/KTU Board-Exam equation** — you must memorize it verbatim.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Directivity of a Half-Wave Dipole

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directivity = (Maximum Radiation Intensity) / (Average Radiation Intensity)**

The radiation intensity is $U(\theta) = \tfrac{r^2}{2\eta_0} \vert E_\theta \vert^2$.

For the half-wave dipole:

$$
U(\theta) = U_0 \left[\frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}\right]^2
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum.** The pattern peaks at $\theta = 90°$ (broadside):

$$
U_{\max} = U_0 \left[\frac{\cos(0)}{\sin(90°)}\right]^2 = U_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1} = U_0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average** by integrating over the full sphere:

$$
\langle U \rangle = \frac{1}{4\pi} \int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^{\pi} U(\theta)\,\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi = \frac{U_0}{2} \int_0^{\pi} \left[\frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}\right]^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.** Using the substitution $u = \cos\theta$:

$$
\int_0^{\pi} \frac{\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}u\right)}{\sin\theta}\, d\theta = \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}u\right)}{1-u^2}\, du
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This integral evaluates to a closed-form constant: $\dfrac{1}{2}[\,\text{Ci}(\pi) - \text{Cin}(\pi)\,] \approx 0.6095$, where $\text{Ci}$ and $\text{Cin}$ are modified Bessel/Cosine integrals.

**Step 4 — Compute the directivity:**

$$
D_0 = \frac{U_{\max}}{\langle U \rangle} = \frac{U_0}{U_0 \times 0.6095} \approx 1.6405
$$

Converting to dBi:

$$
D_0\ (\text{dBi}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.64) \approx \mathbf{2.15\ dBi}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Input Impedance of a Half-Wave Dipole

The driving-point impedance at the center feed is:

$$
Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in} = 73.1 + j\,42.5\ \Omega \quad \text{(at } f = f_r \text{)}
$$

The real part $R_r = 73\ \Omega$ is the **radiation resistance**, and the imaginary part $X_{in} = 42.5\ \Omega$ is a small residual reactance that vanishes when the dipole is shortened by ~5% to account for the end effect (hence $L \approx 0.47\lambda$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Transmission Line Model:**
> $$ Z_{in} = \frac{1}{Y_0} \cdot \frac{Z_T + jZ_0 \tan(\beta L/2)}{Z_0 + jZ_T \tan(\beta L/2)} $$
> where $Z_T$ is the tip impedance (open circuit, $\to \infty$). This reduces to $Z_{in} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta L/2)$, which is purely reactive. The resistive part arises from radiation, modeled as a distributed loss along the transmission line.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define a half-wave dipole antenna. Write the expression for its far-zone electric field component.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A half-wave dipole antenna is a center-fed linear wire antenna whose total physical length is approximately half the operating wavelength ($L \approx 0.47\lambda$ after end-effect correction). The current distribution along its arms follows a cosine law: $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$ for $-\lambda/4 \le z \le \lambda/4$.

The far-zone (Fraunhofer region) electric field component is:

$$
E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0 I_0}{2\pi r}\, \frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}\, e^{-jkr}\quad \text{V/m}
$$

where $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ is the intrinsic impedance of free space, $r$ is the observation distance, $\theta$ is the elevation angle, and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wavenumber. **(3 marks — Full marks for: definition + current expression + E-field formula)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the values of radiation resistance, directivity (in dBi), and HPBW for a standard half-wave dipole antenna.**

| **Directivity ($D_0$)** | **1.64** or equivalently **2.15 dBi** |

The input impedance of a half-wave dipole is $Z_{in} = 73.1 + j42.5\ \Omega$ at the resonant frequency. The HPBW is measured in the elevation plane (E-plane) between the $-3$ dB points of the main lobe. **(3 marks — 1 mark per correct value)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the far-field expression $E_\theta$ of a half-wave dipole antenna, clearly stating the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A half-wave dipole operating at **1 GHz** is simulated in MATLAB. Compute its (i) physical length, (ii) radiation resistance, and (iii) draw/describe the expected 3D radiation pattern with proper labeling of E-plane and H-plane.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions used:**
> 1. Thin-wire approximation ($a \ll \lambda$).
> 2. Sinusoidal current distribution: $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$ for $-\lambda/4 \le z \le \lambda/4$.
> 3. Perfect Electric Conductor (PEC).
> 4. Center-fed with a delta-gap voltage source.
> 5. Far-field observation point: $r \gg 2D^2/\lambda$.

**Derivation (7 Marks — Step-by-step allocation):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The magnetic vector potential at a far-field point due to the line current is:

$$
\mathbf{A}(r) = \hat{z}\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') e^{jkz'\cos\theta}\, dz'
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substituting $I(z') = I_0 \cos(kz')$ and using Euler's identity to expand the cosine, we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \cos(kz') e^{jkz'\cos\theta} dz' 
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\!\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \!\!\left[e^{jkz'(1+\cos\theta)} + e^{jkz'(1-\cos\theta)}\right] dz' \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{kL}{2}\cos\theta\right) \sin\!\left(\tfrac{kL}{2}\right)}{k\sin^2\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Using the far-field relationship $E_\theta = j\omega A_z \sin\theta$ and substituting back, the general expression is:

$$
E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0 I_0}{2\pi r}\, e^{-jkr}\, \left[\frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{kL}{2}\cos\theta\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{kL}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}\right]
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** For the half-wave dipole ($L = \lambda/2 \Rightarrow kL/2 = \pi/2$), $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$, giving the **final expression**:

$$
\boxed{\,E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0 I_0}{2\pi r}\, \frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}\, e^{-jkr}\,}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $f_0 = 1$ GHz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Physical Length (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1 \times 10^9} = 0.3\ \text{m}
$$

Accounting for end-effect correction:

$$
L = 0.47 \times \lambda = 0.47 \times 0.3 = \mathbf{0.141\ \text{m} = 14.1\ \text{cm}}
$$

**(ii) Radiation Resistance (2 marks):**

For a thin half-wave dipole:

$$
R_r = 73\ \Omega
$$

(More precisely, the input impedance is $Z_{in} = 73.1 + j42.5\ \Omega$; the imaginary part cancels when the length is shortened to $0.47\lambda$.)

**(iii) 3D Radiation Pattern Description (3 marks):**

The 3D pattern is **toroidal** ("doughnut-shaped") with the following features:

- **E-plane (Elevation, $\phi = 0°$):** Figure-8 shape with two lobes, nulls along the dipole axis ($\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 180°$), and a maximum at $\theta = 90°$ (broadside).
- **H-plane (Azimuthal, $\theta = 90°$):** Perfect circle — **omnidirectional** (no variation with $\phi$).
- **HPBW:** 78° in the E-plane.
- **First null at:** $\theta \approx \pm 42°$ from broadside.

**Expected simulation results (MATLAB Antenna Toolbox):**
- $S_{11}$ at resonance: $< -10$ dB
- VSWR at resonance: $< 2$
- Directivity: **2.15 dBi**
- Gain: $\approx 2.15$ dBi (100% efficiency assumed)
