### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure to simulate a half-wave dipole antenna using the **Method of Moments (MoM)**. List the outputs obtained from the simulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a dipole antenna, the simulated $S_{11}$ parameter shows a minimum of **$-25$ dB** at **900 MHz** with a **$-10$ dB bandwidth of 80 MHz**. Calculate the (i) VSWR at resonance, (ii) Reflection Coefficient magnitude, (iii) Return Loss in dB, and (iv) the quality factor $Q$ of the antenna.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Method of Moments Simulation Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Geometry Definition:** Define the wire dipole of length $L = 0.47\lambda$, radius $a = 0.005\lambda$, and divide it into $N$ small segments (typically $N \ge 30$ for accuracy).

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Basis Function Selection:** Choose subdomain basis functions — typically **piecewise sinusoidal (PWS)** or **triangular (RWG)** functions — to approximate the unknown current on each segment:

$$
I(z') \approx \sum_{n=1}^{N} I_n \, f_n(z')
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Impedance Matrix Assembly:** Use the **Electric Field Integral Equation (EFIE)** with the free-space Green's function $G(r,r') = \dfrac{e^{-jkR}}{4\pi R}$ to assemble the $N \times N$ impedance matrix:

$$
Z_{mn} = \int\!\!\int f_m(r) \cdot G(r,r') \, f_n(r') \, dr'\, dr
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Excitation Vector:** Define the delta-gap voltage source at the center segment as $V_m$ (nonzero only at the feed segment).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Matrix Solution:** Solve the linear system $[Z][I] = [V]$ using LU decomposition or iterative solvers to obtain the current distribution $I_n$.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Post-Processing Outputs:** Compute the following outputs from the solved currents:
- $S_{11}$ / Return Loss vs. frequency
- VSWR vs. frequency
- Input impedance ($Z_{in}$) on Smith chart
- 2D/3D radiation pattern
- Directivity, gain, HPBW, FBR (front-to-back ratio)
- Surface current distribution

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $S_{11,\min} = -25$ dB at $f_r = 900$ MHz, Bandwidth ($-10$ dB) = 80 MHz.

**(i) VSWR at Resonance (2 marks):**

First, the reflection coefficient magnitude from $S_{11}$ in dB:

$$
S_{11} = 20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert \implies \vert \Gamma \vert = 10^{S_{11}/20} = 10^{-25/20} = 10^{-1.25} \approx 0.0562
$$

VSWR:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + 0.0562}{1 - 0.0562} = \frac{1.0562}{0.9438} \approx 1.119\,}
$$

**(ii) Reflection Coefficient (1 mark):** $\vert \Gamma \vert = 0.0562$ (or $-25$ dB).

**(iii) Return Loss (1 mark):** $RL = 25$ dB (by definition, $RL = -S_{11}$ in dB).

**(iv) Quality Factor (3 marks):**

The fractional bandwidth for a resonant antenna is related to $Q$ by:

$$
\text{FBW} = \frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{Q \cdot \sqrt{\text{VSWR}}}
$$

For VSWR $\approx 1.119$ (very close to 1), we can use the approximation:

$$
Q \approx \frac{f_r}{\Delta f} = \frac{900}{80} = 11.25
$$

Alternatively, using the exact formula:

$$
Q = \frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{\sqrt{\text{VSWR}} \cdot \text{FBW}} = \frac{0.119}{\sqrt{1.119} \cdot (80/900)} = \frac{0.119}{1.0579 \times 0.0889} \approx \mathbf{1.265}
$$

Wait — let me re-evaluate. The correct approximation for narrowband antennas uses:

$$
Q \approx \frac{f_r}{\Delta f_{3\text{dB}}} = \frac{900}{80} = 11.25
$$

The earlier expression with VSWR applies to the *impedance* bandwidth definition. **The correct answer is $Q \approx 11.25$.** (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the End-Effect Correction:** Students often use $L = \lambda/2 = 0.5\lambda$ instead of $L = 0.47\lambda$, losing **1–2 marks** in length calculations.
> 2. **Sign Error in Reflection Coefficient:** Remember $\vert \Gamma \vert = 10^{S_{11}/20}$ uses **divided by 20** (not 10) for voltage ratios.
> 3. **Confusing HPBW with FBR:** HPBW is a 78° figure-8 metric; FBR is the ratio of front lobe to back lobe in directional antennas (not applicable to a simple dipole).
> 4. **Smith Chart Omission:** Always mention the location on the Smith chart (e.g., "$Z_{in}$ is on the right side, slightly above the real axis at $73 + j42.5\ \Omega$").
> 5. **Skipping Units:** Always write dBi for directivity, dB for return loss, Ω for resistance, and m for length.
> 6. **E-field Sign and j-factor:** The factor $j$ in front of $E_\theta$ is a 90° phase shift and **must not be dropped**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 EXAM-DAY QUICK-RECALL FLASHCARD 🚀**

- ✅ **Half-wave dipole length:** $L = 0.47\lambda$ (NOT $0.5\lambda$ — end correction matters).
- ✅ **Current distribution:** $I(z) = I_0 \cos(kz)$ — maximum at center, zero at tips.
- ✅ **E-field equation:** $E_\theta \propto \dfrac{\cos(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta)}{\sin\theta}$ — this is the **most-asked formula**.
- ✅ **Radiation resistance:** **73 Ω** (half-wave) — memorize this constant.
- ✅ **Directivity:** **1.64** (linear) or **2.15 dBi** (logarithmic).
- ✅ **HPBW:** **78°** in the E-plane.
- ✅ **Pattern shape:** **Toroidal / doughnut** in 3D, **figure-8** in E-plane, **circular** in H-plane.
- ✅ **Input impedance:** $Z_{in} = 73.1 + j42.5\ \Omega$ at the geometric center.
- ✅ **Simulation output checklist:** $S_{11}$, VSWR, $Z_{in}$, radiation pattern (2D/3D), directivity, gain, HPBW, FBR.
- ✅ **Numerical method:** Method of Moments (MoM) for wire antennas, FEM for complex 3D structures.
- ✅ **Far-field distance:** $r_{\text{FF}} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ (Fraunhofer distance).
- ✅ **Feed location:** Always at the **center** for a standard half-wave dipole (delta-gap excitation).
- ✅ **Polarization:** **Linear**, aligned with the dipole axis.
- ✅ **Reciprocity:** A dipole used for transmission is identical to one used for reception.
- ✅ **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **assumption** (thin-wire, PEC, sinusoidal current) before derivation — this fetches **bonus marks**.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Simulation of Dipole Antenna**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Patch Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL607 - PCECL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Patch Antenna

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Microstrip Patch Antenna (MPA)** is a low-profile, planar radiating element fabricated by etching the radiating patch on one side of a dielectric substrate whose opposite side is bonded to a continuous metallic ground plane. The patch, typically made of a good conductor such as copper or gold, may take geometric shapes including rectangular, square, triangular, circular, elliptical, or fractal geometries. For a rectangular patch, the dominant resonant mode is **TM₁₀**, where the physical length of the patch is approximately half a guided wavelength ($\lambda_g / 2$) at the operating frequency.

**Simulation of a Patch Antenna** is the computational electromagnetic (CEM) emulation of the antenna's physical structure, excitation, boundary conditions, and radiation behavior using numerical solvers such as the **Method of Moments (MoM)**, **Finite Element Method (FEM)**, or **Finite Integration Technique (FIT)**. The simulation predicts key figures of merit: **return loss (S₁₁)**, **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR)**, **radiation pattern**, **gain**, **directivity**, **bandwidth**, and **input impedance**, without fabricating a physical prototype.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment emphasizes *transmission-line model* based analytical design, followed by full-wave 3D EM solver verification, and validates simulated parameters against theoretical predictions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine throwing a stone into a still pond. The circular ripples spreading outward are similar to electromagnetic waves radiating from the patch antenna. The stone (the patch) is fed energy (from the source), and the energy leaks out as ripples (electromagnetic radiation).

Now think of a **kitchen microwave oven**. It has a metal box (cavity) that traps microwaves and only allows controlled energy to escape. A patch antenna is somewhat the reverse: it is a *controlled leaky cavity*. The patch + substrate + ground plane form a thin **resonant cavity** that stores energy in standing waves between the patch and the ground. At resonance, a small amount of energy deliberately leaks out from the edges of the patch and radiates into free space.

**Simulation**, then, is like a **virtual wind tunnel** for antennas. Just as an aerospace engineer tests aircraft aerodynamics on a computer before building a real plane, an antenna engineer tests the radiation behavior of a patch in software (HFSS, CST, FEKO) before etching copper on a real substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Resonant Frequency ($f_0$):** Frequency at which the antenna naturally radiates most efficiently.
> - **Return Loss ($S_{11}$):** Measured in **dB**, indicates how well the antenna is matched to the feed line. **<-10 dB** is the standard acceptance criterion (implying > 89.9% power radiated).
> - **VSWR:** Ideally **< 2** at resonance.
> - **Bandwidth (BW):** Frequency range where $S_{11} \le -10$ dB.
> - **Gain (dBi):** Power radiated in the boresight direction compared to an isotropic radiator.

## 1.3 Physical Constants Used

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Free-space impedance:** $\eta_0 \approx 377 \, \Omega$
- **Intrinsic impedance of substrate:** $\eta = \eta_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}}}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory of a Microstrip Patch Antenna

The microstrip patch antenna is analyzed through three classical models. KTU board exams typically expect the **Transmission Line Model (TLM)** for design-level questions.

### Step-by-Step Physical Operation

1. **Substrate as Dielectric Spacer:** The dielectric substrate (with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, typically $2.2 \le \varepsilon_r \le 12$) separates the patch from the ground plane, mechanically supporting the structure and allowing fringing fields.

2. **Fringing Fields at Edges:** Because the patch is much smaller than the substrate (electrically thin), the electric fields at the open-circuit edges of the patch do not stop abruptly; they *fringe* into the air, bending outward.

3. **Effective Dielectric Constant:** Due to fringing, the wave propagates partly in the substrate ($\varepsilon_r$) and partly in air ($\varepsilon_0$). This is captured by the **effective dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}}$, which is bounded as $1 < \varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} < \varepsilon_r$.

4. **Resonance:** At the design frequency $f_0$, the patch length $L$ becomes approximately $\lambda_g / 2$, where $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}}}$. A standing wave is established with voltage maxima at the two radiating edges.

5. **Radiation Mechanism:** The two radiating slots (along the width $W$) are separated by $\lambda_g / 2$ and fed in-phase, producing **broadside radiation** (perpendicular to the patch).

6. **Feed Mechanism:** A microstrip line (or coaxial probe) delivers RF current. Impedance matching at the feed point is critical to minimize reflection.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Patch Width $W$ | $W = \dfrac{c}{2 f_0} \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $f_0$ = design frequency, $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity |
| Effective Dielectric Constant $\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}}$ | $\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \left(1 + 12 \dfrac{h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}$ | $h$ = substrate thickness |
| Effective Length $L_{\text{eff}}$ | $L_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}}}}$ | guided half-wavelength |
| Length Extension $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = 0.412 h \dfrac{(\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{r, \text{eff}} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.8)}$ | accounts for fringing |
| Actual Patch Length $L$ | $L = L_{\text{eff}} - 2 \Delta L$ | final etched dimension |
| Ground Plane Length $L_g$ | $L_g = 6 h + L$ | recommended |
| Ground Plane Width $W_g$ | $W_g = 6 h + W$ | recommended |
| Resonant Input Resistance $R_{\text{in}}$ (inset feed) | $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0) \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{L} y_0\right)$ | $y_0$ = inset depth, $R_{\text{in}}(0) \approx 90 \,\Omega$ for $W < \lambda_0$ |
| Return Loss Bandwidth | $BW \approx 3.77 \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{\varepsilon_r^2} \dfrac{W}{L} \dfrac{h}{\lambda_0}$ | percentage bandwidth |
| Fringing Length Correction (Alt. form) | $\Delta L = 0.412 h \dfrac{(\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} + 0.3)}{( \varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} - 0.258 )} \dfrac{(W/h + 0.264)}{(W/h + 0.8)}$ | Hammerschmidt / Balanis form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Microstrip patch antennas dominate **satellite communications (satcom)**, **GPS receivers**, **5G mmWave base stations**, **RFID tags**, **aircraft conformal antennas**, and **biomedical implants** because of their low profile, ease of fabrication using printed-circuit-board (PCB) technology, and conformability to curved surfaces.

## 2.3 Why "Simulation" is Indispensable

- **Cost:** PCB fabrication and anechoic chamber measurement cost orders of magnitude more than a 4-hour simulation run.
- **Parameter Sweep:** $W$, $L$, $h$, $\varepsilon_r$, feed position can be swept in minutes.
- **Insight:** Near-field distributions, surface currents, and 3D polar plots are visualized, which are difficult to measure physically.
- **Optimization:** Tools like HFSS *Optimetrics* and CST *Trust Region Framework* tune dimensions to meet $S_{11}$ and gain specs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Design Example (Worked Model for KTU)

> **Design Specification:**
> - Resonant frequency $f_0 = 2.4 \text{ GHz}$ (Bluetooth / Wi-Fi band)
> - Substrate: RT/Duroid 5880, $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, thickness $h = 1.588 \text{ mm}$
> - Conductivity of patch copper: $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7 \text{ S/m}$

### Step 1 — Patch Width Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{c}{2 f_0}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}} \\
  &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times (2.4 \times 10^{9})}\sqrt{\frac{2}{2.2 + 1}} \\
  &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4.8 \times 10^{9}} \sqrt{\frac{2}{3.2}} \\
  &= 0.0625 \sqrt{0.625} \\
  &= 0.0625 \times 0.7906 \\
  &= 0.04941 \text{ m} \\
  &\approx 49.41 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The width $W$ sets the radiation conductance and is chosen so that the patch supports an efficient TM₁₀ mode. Higher $\varepsilon_r$ yields a narrower $W$.

### Step 2 — Effective Dielectric Constant

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} &= \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + 12\frac{h}{W}\right)^{-1/2} \\
  &= \frac{2.2 + 1}{2} + \frac{2.2 - 1}{2}\left(1 + 12 \times \frac{1.588}{49.41}\right)^{-1/2} \\
  &= 1.6 + 0.6 \left(1 + 0.3856\right)^{-1/2} \\
  &= 1.6 + 0.6 \times (1.3856)^{-1/2} \\
  &= 1.6 + 0.6 \times 0.8497 \\
  &= 1.6 + 0.5098 \\
  &= 2.1098
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The fringing fields extend into the air above the substrate. Hence $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}$ lies between $1$ (pure air) and $\varepsilon_r$ (full substrate). The term $1 + 12h/W$ is empirical (Hammerstad).

### Step 3 — Effective Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{c}{2 f_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}} \\
  &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 2.4 \times 10^{9} \times \sqrt{2.1098}} \\
  &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4.8 \times 10^{9} \times 1.4525} \\
  &= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{6.972 \times 10^{9}} \\
  &= 0.04303 \text{ m} \\
  &= 43.03 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The guided wavelength $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}$ shrinks because of the dielectric loading; the half-wavelength therefore becomes smaller than the free-space half-wavelength of $62.5$ mm.

### Step 4 — Length Extension due to Fringing

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta L}{h} &= 0.412 \times \frac{(\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.8)} \\
  &= 0.412 \times \frac{(2.1098 + 0.3)(49.41/1.588 + 0.264)}{(2.1098 - 0.258)(49.41/1.588 + 0.8)} \\
  &= 0.412 \times \frac{(2.4098)(31.117 + 0.264)}{(1.8518)(31.117 + 0.8)} \\
  &= 0.412 \times \frac{2.4098 \times 31.381}{1.8518 \times 31.917} \\
  &= 0.412 \times \frac{75.620}{59.114} \\
  &= 0.412 \times 1.2793 \\
  &= 0.5271
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= 0.5271 \times h = 0.5271 \times 1.588 = 0.8371 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Fringing makes the patch *electrically* longer than its *physical* length. We compensate by *reducing* $L$ to maintain the same electrical length.

### Step 5 — Actual Patch Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= L_{\text{eff}} - 2 \Delta L \\
  &= 43.03 - 2 \times 0.8371 \\
  &= 43.03 - 1.6742 \\
  &= 41.356 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Ground Plane Dimensions

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_g &= 6h + L = 6 \times 1.588 + 41.356 = 9.528 + 41.356 = 50.884 \text{ mm} \\
W_g &= 6h + W = 6 \times 1.588 + 49.41 = 9.528 + 49.41 = 58.938 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Final Designed Values

| **Parameter** | **Value** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Resonant Frequency $f_0$ | 2.4 | GHz |
| Substrate $\varepsilon_r$ | 2.2 | — |
| Substrate Height $h$ | 1.588 | mm |
| Patch Width $W$ | 49.41 | mm |
| Effective Permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}$ | 2.1098 | — |
| Effective Length $L_{\text{eff}}$ | 43.03 | mm |
| Length Extension $\Delta L$ | 0.8371 | mm |
| Patch Length $L$ | 41.36 | mm |
| Ground Plane Length $L_g$ | 50.88 | mm |
| Ground Plane Width $W_g$ | 58.94 | mm |
| Expected BW (formula) | $\approx 2.0$ | % |
| Inset Feed Depth $y_0$ (for 50 $\Omega$) | $\approx 9.6$ | mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Geometry Block Diagram

A patch antenna is composed of **four layers** stacked vertically: a top patch, a dielectric substrate, a ground plane, and an optional feed line on the same plane as the patch. The figure below maps the physical architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Layer - Radiating Patch - Copper] --> B[Dielectric Substrate - eps_r - h]
    B --> C[Ground Plane - Continuous Copper Sheet]
    C --> D[Feed Line - Microstrip Line - 50 ohm - Top Layer]
    D --> E[Feed Point - Inset or Probe - at y0]
    E --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid-Compiled Simulation Workflow Block Diagram

The KTU lab workflow for the simulation experiment proceeds through a tightly defined pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Define Design Specifications - f0 - BW - eps_r - h] --> P2[Step 2: Analytical TLM Calculation - W - L - DeltaL - Lg - Wg]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Open EM Solver - HFSS or CST Studio Suite]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Create 3D Model - Substrate - Patch - Ground - Feedline]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Assign Boundary Conditions - Radiation Enclosure - Perfect E on Ground]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Assign Excitation - Lumped Port or Wave Port - 50 ohm]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Define Frequency Sweep - 2 GHz to 3 GHz - Step 0.01 GHz]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Mesh Generation - Adaptive Lambda Refinement]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Run S Parameter Simulation]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Post Process - S11 Plot - VSWR - Impedance Smith Chart]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Run Far Field Simulation at f0]
    P11 --> P12[Step 12: Post Process - 3D Gain Pattern - E and H plane 2D cuts]
    P12 --> P13[Step 13: Compare with Analytical - Compute Percentage Error]
    P13 --> P14[Step 14: Optional Parametric Optimization - Inset feed depth - L - W]
    P14 --> P15[Step 15: Final Report - Pass if S11 below minus 10 dB at f0]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Design Inputs]
        I1[Resonant Frequency f0]
        I2[Substrate eps_r]
        I3[Substrate Height h]
        I4[Feed Type - Inset - Probe - Aperture]
    end
    subgraph CALC[Analytical Engine TLM]
        C1[Width W Computation]
        C2[Effective eps_reff]
        C3[DeltaL Computation]
        C4[Length L Computation]
    end
    subgraph SOLVER[3D EM Solver]
        S1[Geometry Editor]
        S2[Boundary and Excitation]
        S3[Adaptive Mesh]
        S4[Solver Run - FEM - MoM - FIT]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Simulation Outputs]
        O1[Return Loss S11 in dB]
        O2[VSWR vs Frequency]
        O3[Input Impedance on Smith Chart]
        O4[3D Radiation Pattern and Gain dBi]
        O5[E Plane and H Plane 2D Cuts]
        O6[Bandwidth and Directivity]
    end
    INPUT --> CALC
    CALC --> SOLVER
    SOLVER --> OUTPUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions in Simulation:**
> - *Radiation Boundary* (HFSS) or *Open Add Space* (CST) surrounds the antenna to absorb outgoing waves.
> - *Perfect Electric Conductor (PEC)* is applied to the patch and ground plane.
> - *Wave Port* is the standard excitation to model a $50 \, \Omega$ transmission line.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a microstrip patch antenna. Mention any three advantages of microstrip patch antennas.**

**Model Answer:**
A microstrip patch antenna is a low-profile printed antenna consisting of a metallic patch etched on a dielectric substrate backed by a ground plane. Three advantages are:
1. **Low profile and lightweight**, making them suitable for aerospace and conformal applications.
2. **Ease of fabrication** using standard photolithographic PCB processes, enabling low-cost mass production.
3. **Conformal to curved surfaces** such as aircraft fuselages and biomedical implants.

> [!TIP]
> Always mention the substrate role in your definition; the examiner allocates 1 mark specifically for the substrate-ground-plane description.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the effective dielectric constant of a microstrip antenna? Why is it less than the substrate permittivity?**

**Model Answer:**
The effective dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}$ is the equivalent permittivity experienced by the EM wave as it propagates partly through the substrate and partly through the air, due to fringing fields. It is less than $\varepsilon_r$ because part of the field energy resides in the air region above the substrate, which has a lower permittivity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the design equations for the length and width of a rectangular microstrip patch antenna using the transmission line model. Clearly state the role of fringing fields. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The transmission-line model treats the patch as two parallel radiating slots, each of width $W$ and height $h$, separated by a low-impedance transmission line of length $L$.

**Width derivation (energy conservation):** The width is chosen so the patch radiates efficiently. The standard formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W = \frac{c}{2 f_0}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective permittivity derivation (fringing inclusion):** The fringing fields make the effective electrical length longer than the physical length. Hammerstad's empirical expression is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + 12\frac{h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective length (resonance condition):** For TM₁₀ mode,

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\lambda_g}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fringing length correction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L = 0.412 h \frac{(\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.8)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final physical length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L = L_{\text{eff}} - 2 \Delta L
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor of 2 arises because **fringing occurs at both open-circuited ends** of the patch.

**(b)** Design a rectangular microstrip patch antenna for $f_0 = 2.4$ GHz on a substrate of $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$ and $h = 1.588$ mm. Compute $W$, $L$, $\Delta L$, and ground plane dimensions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:** *(See SECTION 3 worked example.)*

- $W = 49.41$ mm
- $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} = 2.1098$
- $L_{\text{eff}} = 43.03$ mm
- $\Delta L = 0.837$ mm
- $L = 41.36$ mm
- $L_g = 50.88$ mm, $W_g = 58.94$ mm

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for simulating a microstrip patch antenna using HFSS or any 3D EM solver. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Project Initialization:** Open HFSS → *Insert HFSS Design*. Set solution type to *Modal Network Analysis*.
2. **3D Geometry Creation:** Draw the dielectric substrate (Box), patch (Rectangle), ground plane (Rectangle), and microstrip feed line.
3. **Material Assignment:** Substrate = RT/Duroid ($\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, $\tan \delta = 0.0009$). Patch and ground = copper. Feed = Perfect Conductor.
4. **Boundary Conditions:** Assign *Perfect E* to the patch and ground; *Radiation Boundary* to an airbox enclosing the antenna.
5. **Excitation:** Insert a *Lumped Port* (for probe feed) or *Wave Port* (for microstrip line) at the feed end, integrating the modal line by drawing a perpendicular rectangle of length $\approx 4h$.
6. **Solution Setup:** Add a frequency sweep from 2 GHz to 3 GHz with 0.01 GHz step.
7. **Mesh:** Use *Initial Mesh* + *Adaptive Mesh Refinement*; convergence target $\Delta S_{11} \le 0.02$ for two consecutive passes.
8. **Validation:** Run *Validation Check* (no overlapping conductors, port inside airbox).
9. **Simulation Run:** Click *Analyze All*.
10. **Post-Processing:** Plot $S_{11}$ (Rectangular Plot → dB), Smith chart, 3D polar radiation pattern, and 2D E-plane and H-plane cuts at $f_0$.

**(b)** List the simulation outputs that are typically extracted from a patch antenna simulation, and state the ideal acceptance criteria for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Output Parameter** | **Acceptance Criterion** | **Marks Allocated** |
|---|---|---|
| Return Loss $S_{11}$ | $\le -10$ dB at $f_0$ | 2 |
| VSWR | $\le 2:1$ at $f_0$ | 1 |
| Bandwidth | $S_{11} \le -10$ dB range | 1 |
| Input Impedance | $\approx 50 \, \Omega$ (resistive) at $f_0$ | 1 |
| Radiation Pattern | Broadside (E-plane and H-plane) | 1 |
| Gain (dBi) | Application-dependent, typically 5–8 dBi | 1 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\Delta L$ term** in $L$ calculation is the single most common error, leading to a resonant frequency *lower* than the design value. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing free-space wavelength with guided wavelength** — students write $L = \lambda_0 / 2$ without $\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}$, leading to a $40\%$ design error.
> 3. **Stating only "broadside"** for radiation pattern without naming *E-plane* and *H-plane* loses 1 mark.
> 4. **In simulation workflow questions**, students forget the *validation check* step before running analysis, which examiners specifically look for in HFSS/CST workflow questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Patch Antenna = Printed Antenna** = Patch + Substrate + Ground Plane. Always mention all three layers in definitions.
- **Dominant Mode:** TM₁₀ for rectangular patch. Length $L$ controls resonance; width $W$ controls radiation conductance and bandwidth.
- **Fringing Effect:** Two consequences — (i) effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} < \varepsilon_r$, and (ii) effective electrical length $L_{\text{eff}} > L$.
- **Key Formula Trio to Memorize:**
  - $W = \dfrac{c}{2f_0}\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_r+1}}$
  - $\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r+1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r-1}{2}\left(1+12h/W\right)^{-1/2}$
  - $L = \dfrac{c}{2f_0\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}} - 2\Delta L$
- **Simulation Acceptance Criteria:** $S_{11} \le -10$ dB, VSWR $\le 2$, gain and pattern consistent with analytical expectations (broadside).
- **Ground Plane Rule:** $L_g \ge L + 6h$ and $W_g \ge W + 6h$ — violating this causes edge diffraction and pattern distortion in simulation.
- **Feed Techniques:** Inset feed (easiest, planar), coaxial probe (broadband but requires drilling), aperture-coupled (highest BW, multi-layer).
- **Inset Feed Matching Rule of Thumb:** Inset depth $y_0 \approx 0.3 L$ gives approximately $50 \, \Omega$ feed-point impedance for typical $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.2$.
- **Mesh Convergence:** Always run at least 2 adaptive passes; for high-Q antennas, target $\Delta S_{11} \le 0.01$ between consecutive passes.
- **Tools Tested in KTU Labs:** HFSS, CST Studio Suite, FEKO, ADS Momentum, IE3D, MATLAB Antenna Toolbox.
- **Exam-Favorite Numbers:** $f_0 = 2.4$ GHz (Wi-Fi), $f_0 = 5.8$ GHz (ISM), $f_0 = 1.575$ GHz (GPS L1), $f_0 = 2.45$ GHz (RFID) — be ready to plug any of these into the design equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Revision Punchline:**
> *"Simulating a patch antenna = analytically sizing the patch (W, L, ΔL) from $f_0$, $\varepsilon_r$, $h$ using the Transmission Line Model → building a 3D model in HFSS/CST → applying radiation boundaries and a wave port → solving Maxwell's equations via FEM/MoM → verifying $S_{11} \le -10$ dB and broadside radiation."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of Antenna Array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL607 - Antenna and Wave Propagation Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of Antenna Array

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Antenna Array** is a system of two or more spatially distributed radiating elements (antennas) that are excited by coherent RF signals with controlled relative amplitudes and phases, such that the radiated fields superpose in the far-field region to produce a desired directional radiation pattern.

Mathematically, the total field of an array in the far-field is expressed by **Pattern Multiplication Theorem**:

$$E_{\text{total}}(\theta,\phi) = E_{\text{element}}(\theta,\phi) \times AF(\theta,\phi)$$

where $AF(\theta,\phi)$ is the **Array Factor**, which depends only on the geometry and excitation of the array, while $E_{\text{element}}$ is the individual element pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCECL607)**
> The experiment on "Simulation of Antenna Array" is mapped to **CO2 / CO3 (Apply & Analyze)** under the **NEP 2020 lab rubric**. Students must simulate Uniform Linear Arrays (ULA), observe beam-steering, and verify Array Factor plots against analytical expressions. Marks are awarded for both **theoretical validation** and **plot interpretation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Synchronized Choir

Imagine a choir of **N singers** standing in a straight line. If each singer sings the same note with **identical loudness and timing**, the sound radiates almost equally in all directions (omnidirectional). But if:

- We **delay** the singing of each subsequent singer by a small amount (phase shift), the sound waves from all singers **add constructively** in one specific direction and **cancel** in others. This is **beam steering**.
- We **make the middle singers louder** than the edge ones (amplitude tapering), the main beam becomes narrower and side lobes reduce. This is **amplitude weighting** (e.g., Taylor, Chebyshev distributions).

This is exactly how an antenna array works — the spatial position, amplitude, and phase of each radiating element determine the far-field radiation pattern.

## 1.3 Types of Antenna Arrays (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Array Type | Geometry | Typical Application |
|------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Uniform Linear Array (ULA)** | N elements on a straight line, equal spacing $d$ | Radar, base-station antennas |
| **Planar Array** | Elements on a 2D grid | Phased-array radars, satellite tracking |
| **Circular Array** | Elements on a circle | Omnidirectional scanning |
| **Conformal Array** | Elements on a curved surface | Aircraft/vehicle skins |

## 1.4 Why Simulate Antenna Arrays?

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Bold:**
> - **HPBW (Half-Power Beamwidth)** — angular width between $-3\,\text{dB}$ points of the main lobe.
> - **SLL (Side Lobe Level)** — ratio of the highest side lobe peak to the main lobe peak (in dB).
> - **Directivity (D)** — ratio of maximum radiation intensity to average radiation intensity.
> - **Array Factor (AF)** — complex sum of element excitations with phase terms.
> - **Scan Angle ($\theta_0$)** — direction of the main beam maximum.

Simulation allows engineers to predict the radiation pattern *before* fabrication, saving cost and time. Tools like **MATLAB (Phased Array Toolbox)**, **4nec2**, **CST Studio Suite**, and **HFSS** are industry standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Building Blocks of an Array

The far-field pattern of any array can be broken down into three independent decisions:

1. **Geometry** — where each element is placed (linear, planar, circular).
2. **Excitation (Amplitude)** — how strongly each element is fed ($a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{N-1}$).
3. **Excitation (Phase)** — the progressive phase shift $\beta$ between adjacent elements.

Because of the **Pattern Multiplication Theorem**, the total pattern is the *product* of the *element pattern* and the *array factor*. For identical elements, the array factor *alone* dictates the shape of the main beam, side lobes, and nulls.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Most exam problems assume **isotropic elements** ($E_{\text{element}} = 1$), so the total pattern = Array Factor. This simplifies derivations drastically.

## 2.2 N-Element Uniform Linear Array (ULA) — Step Logic

For an N-element ULA placed along the **z-axis** with uniform spacing $d$ and progressive phase $\beta$:

- Element $n$ is located at position $z_n = n\,d$, for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$.
- The path difference between element $n$ and element $0$, as seen from a far-field observer at angle $\theta$, is $\Delta r_n = n\,d\cos\theta$.
- This path difference introduces a phase term $e^{j k n d \cos\theta}$ in the radiated field.
- The element also carries a feed phase shift $\beta_n = n\beta$, contributing $e^{j n \beta}$.

The **Array Factor** is the sum over all elements:

$$AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n \, e^{j n \psi}$$

where $\psi = k d \cos\theta + \beta$ is the **total inter-element phase progression**, with $k = 2\pi/\lambda$.

For a **uniform array** ($a_n = 1$ for all $n$), this becomes a finite geometric series.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE and lab viva.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Wave number | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | rad/m |
| 2 | Array Factor (general) | $AF(\theta) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n e^{j n (k d \cos\theta + \beta)}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Uniform Linear Array AF | $AF(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{N\psi}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)} \, e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}$ | closed form |
| 4 | Inter-element phase | $\psi = k d \cos\theta + \beta$ | rad |
| 5 | Main beam direction | $\theta_0 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-\beta}{k d}\right)$ | rad / deg |
| 6 | HPBW (broadside, large N) | $HPBW \approx \dfrac{0.886 \,\lambda}{N d \cos\theta_0}$ | radians |
| 7 | First null beamwidth | $FNBW = \dfrac{2\lambda}{N d}$ | radians |
| 8 | Directivity (broadside ULA) | $D_0 \approx \dfrac{2 N d}{\lambda}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | SLL (uniform ULA) | $-13.2\,\text{dB}$ | first side lobe |
| 10 | No grating-lobe condition | $d < \dfrac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}$ | meters |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` (a LaTeX vertical bar) to keep the markdown table rendering safely.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G/6G base stations** use linear/planar phased arrays for *beamforming* and *MIMO*.
- **Radar systems** (e.g., AN/TPY-2, AWACS) use thousands of elements to track ballistic missiles.
- **Radio astronomy** (e.g., VLA in New Mexico) uses interferometric arrays to image distant galaxies with angular resolution better than $0.1$ arcseconds.
- **Satellite communication** on moving platforms (boats, aircraft) uses conformal arrays to maintain link with geostationary satellites.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the N-Element ULA Array Factor

**Step 1 — Geometry and Field Contribution of Element $n$.**

Place the array along the z-axis. Element $n$ (with $n=0,1,\dots,N-1$) is at position $z_n = n\,d$. For a far-field observer at angle $\theta$ (measured from the z-axis), the distance from element $n$ to the observer is approximately $r_n \approx r - n d \cos\theta$.

The complex phasor contribution of element $n$ to the far electric field is:

$$E_n \propto a_n \, e^{j n \beta} \cdot \frac{e^{-j k r_n}}{r_n} \approx a_n \, e^{j n \beta} \cdot \frac{e^{-j k r}}{r} \cdot e^{+j k n d \cos\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Summing All Elements (Pattern Multiplication).**

Since the $1/r$ factor is common, the *array factor* is defined as the sum of the directional phase terms:

$$AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n \, e^{j n (k d \cos\theta + \beta)}$$

For **uniform excitation** ($a_n = 1$ for all $n$):

$$AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi}, \quad \text{where} \quad \psi = k d \cos\theta + \beta$$

**Step 3 — Geometric-Series Summation.**

Use the standard identity:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^n = \frac{1 - x^N}{1 - x}$$

Setting $x = e^{j\psi}$:

$$AF(\theta) = \frac{1 - e^{j N \psi}}{1 - e^{j \psi}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Sinc (Dirichlet) Form.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugates $e^{-j N \psi/2}$ and $e^{-j \psi/2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
AF(\theta) &= \frac{e^{-j N \psi/2} - e^{+j N \psi/2}}{e^{-j \psi/2} - e^{+j \psi/2}} \cdot \frac{e^{j N \psi/2}}{e^{j \psi/2}} \\
&= \frac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{N \psi}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)} \cdot e^{j (N-1)\psi/2}
\end{aligned}$$

The **magnitude** is the well-known **Dirichlet kernel**:

$$\boxed{\;\vert AF(\theta) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{N \psi}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)} \right\vert\;}$$

**Step 5 — Conditions for Main-Lobe Maxima and Nulls.**

- **Main lobe maxima** occur when $\psi = 2m\pi$ for $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$
  - The **broadside direction** ($\theta = 90^\circ$) is achieved when $\beta = 0$.
- **Nulls** occur when $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$ but $\sin(\psi/2) \neq 0$, i.e., $\psi = \dfrac{2 m \pi}{N}$ for $m = 1, 2, \dots, N-1$.
- **Grating lobes** appear when $|\psi| > 2\pi$. To avoid them in visible space ($\theta \in [0,\pi]$), enforce $d < \lambda$.

**Step 6 — Beam-Steering (Scanning) Derivation.**

Setting $\psi = 0$ for the desired look-direction $\theta_0$:

$$0 = k d \cos\theta_0 + \beta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\;\beta = -k d \cos\theta_0\;}$$

Substituting back, the inter-element phase required to steer the beam to $\theta_0$ is linear in $\cos\theta_0$.

**Step 7 — Directivity of Broadside ULA.**

For a uniform broadside ULA with $N \gg 1$ and $d = \lambda/2$:

$$D_0 = \frac{2 N d}{\lambda} = N$$

This is the classic result that **directivity grows linearly with N** for half-wavelength spacing.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 4-element ULA has $d = \lambda/2$, $\beta = 0$ (broadside). Plot the AF and find the HPBW.

**Step 1.** Set $\psi = k d \cos\theta = \pi \cos\theta$ (since $kd = \pi$).

**Step 2.** Compute $\vert AF \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(2\pi \cos\theta)}{\sin(\pi \cos\theta/2)} \right\vert$.

**Step 3.** Main beam at $\theta = 90^\circ$ gives $\cos\theta = 0$, so $\psi = 0$ and $\vert AF \vert = 4$ (since $\lim_{x\to 0} \sin(Nx)/\sin(x) = N$).

**Step 4.** First nulls at $\psi = \pm \pi/2$, i.e., $\cos\theta = \pm 0.5$, so $\theta_1 = 60^\circ$ and $\theta_2 = 120^\circ$.

**Step 5.** HPBW $\approx 50.6^\circ$ (from $-3\,\text{dB}$ points), and SLL $\approx -13.2\,\text{dB}$.

**Step 6.** For $\theta_0 = 30^\circ$ (beam steering), set $\beta = -\pi \cos 30^\circ = -\dfrac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx -2.72\,\text{rad}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Antenna Array Physical Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[RF Source] --> sp[1-to-N Power Splitter]
    sp --> psh1[Phase Shifter beta equals 0]
    sp --> psh2[Phase Shifter beta equals 1]
    sp --> psh3[Phase Shifter beta equals 2]
    sp --> pshN[Phase Shifter beta equals N-1]
    psh1 --> a1[Antenna Element 0 at z equals 0]
    psh2 --> a2[Antenna Element 1 at z equals d]
    psh3 --> a3[Antenna Element 2 at z equals 2d]
    pshN --> aN[Antenna Element N-1 at z equals N-1 times d]
    a1 --> ff[Far Field Superposition]
    a2 --> ff
    a3 --> ff
    aN --> ff
    ff --> pat[Desired Radiation Pattern]
```

## 4.2 Simulation Workflow (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Array Parameters N, d, lambda, beta] --> B[Compute psi for theta in 0 to 180 deg]
    B --> C[Evaluate AF magnitude using Dirichlet formula]
    C --> D[Convert AF to dB scale 20 log10 AF]
    D --> E[Find Main Lobe Peak and theta0]
    E --> F[Locate Nulls and Compute HPBW]
    F --> G[Compute SLL from first side lobe]
    G --> H[Plot Normalized Pattern in dB vs theta]
    H --> I[Validate Against Analytical Theory]
    I --> J{Does Pattern Match?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Experiment Complete: Record Results]
    J -- No --> L[Check d less than lambda and beta values]
    L --> A
```

## 4.3 Pattern-Multiplication Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    elem[Element Pattern E sub element] --> mul[Multiplication Block]
    af[Array Factor AF computed from geometry] --> mul
    mul --> total[Total Array Pattern E sub total]
    note1[For isotropic elements E sub element equals 1] -.-> elem
    note2[AF depends only on geometry, amplitude, phase] -.-> af
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the lab record:** When generating polar plots, normalize the AF to its maximum ($0\,\text{dB}$ at the main lobe peak) and use a step size of $\Delta\theta = 0.5^\circ$ for smooth curves.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define *Array Factor* of an antenna array. Why is the pattern multiplication theorem important in array analysis?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Array Factor (AF) is a complex function that describes how the far-field pattern of an array depends on the **geometry, amplitude weights, and phase shifts** of the individual elements, assuming isotropic radiators. Mathematically,

$$AF(\theta,\phi) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n \, e^{j n \psi}$$

The **Pattern Multiplication Theorem** states that the total field of an array = (element pattern) × (array factor). Its importance lies in **decoupling the analysis** — engineers can optimize the array geometry independently of the element design, drastically simplifying the synthesis of complex radiation patterns (1 mark for AF definition, 2 marks for theorem and its significance).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** State and explain the condition to avoid grating lobes in a uniform linear array. What is the maximum allowed element spacing for a broadside array?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Grating lobes are unwanted additional maxima in the radiation pattern that appear when the inter-element phase $\psi$ exceeds $2\pi$ in visible space. To avoid them, the spacing must satisfy

$$d < \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}$$

For a **broadside** array ($\theta_0 = 90^\circ$, so $\cos\theta_0 = 0$), the condition reduces to $d < \lambda$. The **maximum safe spacing** is conventionally taken as $d = \lambda/2$ to provide a margin against scan-induced grating lobes (1 mark for grating-lobe concept, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for broadside case).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Array Factor of an N-element Uniform Linear Array (ULA) with element spacing $d$ and progressive phase shift $\beta$. Show that the AF magnitude reduces to the Dirichlet kernel form. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

1. *(1 mark)* Place the array along the z-axis. Element $n$ at $z_n = n d$. For a far-field observer at angle $\theta$, the path difference is $n d \cos\theta$, contributing a phase $e^{j k n d \cos\theta}$.

2. *(1 mark)* Each element has a feed phase $n\beta$, contributing $e^{j n\beta}$. The total inter-element phase is

$$\psi = k d \cos\theta + \beta$$

3. *(1 mark)* The Array Factor for uniform excitation ($a_n = 1$) is the geometric sum:

$$AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi}$$

4. *(2 marks)* Apply the geometric-series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^n = (1-x^N)/(1-x)$ with $x = e^{j\psi}$:

$$AF(\theta) = \frac{1 - e^{j N \psi}}{1 - e^{j \psi}}$$

5. *(2 marks)* Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-j N \psi/2}$ and $e^{-j\psi/2}$ to obtain the Dirichlet form:

$$AF(\theta) = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \, e^{j (N-1)\psi/2}$$

Hence the magnitude is

$$\vert AF(\theta) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$$

---

**(b)** For a 4-element broadside ULA with $d = \lambda/2$, compute the HPBW and SLL. If the beam is steered to $\theta_0 = 30^\circ$, find the required progressive phase shift $\beta$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

1. *(1 mark)* Broadside condition: $\beta = 0$, so $\psi = \pi \cos\theta$ (since $kd = \pi$).

2. *(1 mark)* Main beam at $\theta = 90^\circ$: $\psi = 0$, $\vert AF \vert = 4$ (max).

3. *(1 mark)* First nulls at $\psi = \pm \pi/2$, giving $\cos\theta = \pm 0.5$, so $\theta_1 = 60^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 120^\circ$.

4. *(1 mark)* HPBW is found by solving $\vert AF \vert = 4/\sqrt{2}$ numerically, giving approximately $\theta \approx 64.7^\circ$ and $115.3^\circ$, so HPBW $\approx 50.6^\circ$.

5. *(1 mark)* SLL is the value of the first side lobe at $\psi \approx 4.49$ rad, which gives $\vert AF \vert / 4 \approx 0.213$, i.e., $\text{SLL} = 20 \log_{10}(0.213) \approx -13.2\,\text{dB}$.

6. *(2 marks)* Beam steering: set $\beta = -k d \cos\theta_0 = -\pi \cos 30^\circ = -\dfrac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{2} \approx -2.72\,\text{rad} \approx -156^\circ$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *beam steering* in a phased array. Derive the expression for progressive phase shift $\beta$ required to scan the main beam to an angle $\theta_0$ from broadside. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

1. *(2 marks)* Beam steering is the electronic redirection of the array's main lobe by varying the progressive phase shift $\beta$ between adjacent elements, without physically moving the array.

2. *(2 marks)* The main beam occurs when $\psi = 0$:

$$k d \cos\theta_0 + \beta = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \beta = -k d \cos\theta_0$$

3. *(1 mark)* Substituting $k = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$\beta = -\frac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0$$

4. *(1 mark)* Example: for $d = \lambda/2$ and $\theta_0 = 60^\circ$, $\beta = -\pi \cdot 0.5 = -90^\circ$.

5. *(1 mark)* For endfire ($\theta_0 = 0^\circ$), $\beta = -2\pi d/\lambda$.

---

**(b)** Discuss the effect of element spacing $d$ on the radiation pattern of a ULA. What happens when $d \geq \lambda$? Plot (sketch) the AF for $d = \lambda/2$ and $d = \lambda$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (b):**

1. *(2 marks)* For $d < \lambda/2$, the array is undersampled and the main lobe is wide with low directivity.

2. *(2 marks)* For $d = \lambda/2$ (standard), the array has the **narrowest main lobe without grating lobes** for broadside; directivity $D_0 \approx N$.

3. *(2 marks)* For $d \geq \lambda$, **grating lobes** appear — additional maxima of equal height to the main lobe, causing ambiguity in direction finding and wasted radiated power.

4. *(1 mark)* Sketch (textual description): at $d = \lambda/2$, a single main lobe at $90^\circ$ with two small side lobes ($\approx -13.2\,\text{dB}$). At $d = \lambda$, a main lobe at $90^\circ$ **plus** grating lobes at $0^\circ$ and $180^\circ$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Antenna Array Questions**
> 1. **Confusing $\theta$ convention** — Some textbooks use $\theta$ from the *broadside* direction, others from the *array axis*. KTU uses $\theta$ from the **z-axis (array axis)**. Always state your convention explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\vert \cdot \vert$ in AF magnitude** — Writing $AF = \sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2)$ without absolute-value bars loses 1 mark. The AF is real-valued *only* when $\psi$ is real and the ratio is positive; the absolute value handles negative values.
> 3. **SLL sign error** — Side Lobe Level is always **negative** (e.g., $-13.2\,\text{dB}$). Writing it as positive is incorrect.
> 4. **Ignoring the $(N-1)$ phase factor** — The AF has a phase term $e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}$ representing the *array phase center*; omitting it loses marks in derivations.
> 5. **Plot not normalized** — Always normalize to the main-lobe peak ($0\,\text{dB}$) before plotting in dB.
> 6. **Units inconsistency** — Use **radians** in all formulas, then convert angles to **degrees** only at the plotting/result stage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Antenna Array** = multiple coherent radiators with controlled amplitude/phase.
- ✅ **Pattern Multiplication Theorem**: $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{element}} \times AF$.
- ✅ **Array Factor (ULA)**: $AF(\theta) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n e^{j n \psi}$, with $\psi = k d \cos\theta + \beta$.
- ✅ **Uniform ULA closed form**: $\vert AF \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$ (Dirichlet kernel).
- ✅ **Beam-steering formula**: $\beta = -k d \cos\theta_0 = -\dfrac{2\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta_0$.
- ✅ **No grating-lobe condition**: $d < \lambda / (1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert)$.
- ✅ **HPBW (ULA, broadside)**: $\approx 0.886 \lambda / (N d)$ radians.
- ✅ **Directivity (ULA, broadside, $d=\lambda/2$)**: $D_0 \approx N$.
- ✅ **SLL of uniform ULA**: $-13.2\,\text{dB}$ (fixed, regardless of $N$).
- ✅ **Broadside**: $\beta = 0$, $\theta_0 = 90^\circ$.
- ✅ **Endfire**: $\beta = \pm k d$, $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$.
- ✅ **Simulation steps**: define $N, d, \lambda, \beta$ → compute $\psi(\theta)$ → evaluate Dirichlet AF → normalize → plot in dB → measure HPBW & SLL.
- ✅ **Tools**: MATLAB Phased Array Toolbox, 4nec2, CST, HFSS, Python-`NumPy` (theoretical) — but **no Python code in the answer sheet** unless explicitly permitted by the lab instructor.
- ✅ **For KTU viva**: be ready to explain *why* $d = \lambda/2$ is chosen (trade-off between directivity and grating-lobe margin) and *what happens* if amplitudes are tapered (SLL decreases, HPBW increases).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Vector Network Analyzer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL607 - PCECL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Vector Network Analyzer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)** is a precision electronic test and measurement instrument used in radio frequency (RF), microwave, and millimeter-wave engineering to characterize the **linear behavior of electrical networks** by measuring both the **magnitude and phase** of incident, reflected, and transmitted wave quantities. Unlike a scalar network analyzer that only measures amplitude, the VNA provides the complete **complex (vector) scattering parameters (S-parameters)** of the Device Under Test (DUT).

In the KTU 2024 scheme laboratory context, the VNA is studied as the **primary instrument for characterizing two-port networks** such as filters, amplifiers, attenuators, antennas, and transmission line sections over a swept frequency range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The experiment emphasizes hands-on operation of the VNA, calibration procedures (SOLT – Short, Open, Load, Thru), measurement of S11, S21, S22, S12, return loss, insertion loss, VSWR, and 1 dB compression point characterization of RF devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at one end of a long, narrow **echoey tunnel** (the transmission line) and you shout a loud, pure whistle at a specific pitch (the **incident signal** at a single frequency). You then place different obstacles in the tunnel (the **DUT** — like a filter, amplifier, or mismatched load) and listen carefully:

- **How much of your whistle comes back to you?** → This tells you the **reflection** (related to **S11** or input reflection coefficient).
- **How much of the whistle reaches the other end of the tunnel, and how delayed is it?** → This tells you the **transmission** (related to **S21** or forward gain/loss) and the **phase delay**.

The VNA is essentially an **"intelligent whistling listener"** that can:
1. **Generate** a precisely known signal (the incident wave `a1`).
2. **Separate and measure** both the wave that bounced back (`b1`) and the wave that passed through (`b2`).
3. **Compare the ratio** (`b1/a1`, `b2/a1`) in both **magnitude (dB)** and **phase (degrees)** across a swept range of frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Vector"?** The word *vector* refers to the fact that the VNA captures both the **magnitude** AND the **phase** of the signal, not just the power. This is critical because impedance, group delay, and phase response of RF circuits cannot be determined from magnitude alone.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Terminology

The following metrics are routinely reported by a VNA:

- **S-parameters (Scattering Parameters):** Complex dimensionless ratios defining network behavior.
- **Return Loss (RL):** Expressed in **dB**; quantifies how much power is reflected back from the DUT input.
- **Insertion Loss (IL):** Expressed in **dB**; quantifies how much signal power is lost as the wave passes through the DUT.
- **VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio):** Dimensionless ratio, always **≥ 1**.
- **Group Delay (τg):** Expressed in **seconds** (typically ns or ps); the rate of change of phase with respect to frequency.
- **Smith Chart Plot:** A polar plot normalized to the **characteristic impedance Z₀ = 50 Ω**.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference Impedance:** Almost all VNAs are calibrated for a system impedance of **Z₀ = 50 Ω** (the industry standard for RF/microwave systems). Some specialized systems use **75 Ω** (video/cable TV).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind the VNA: Scattering Parameters

Electrical networks operating at low frequencies are typically described by **impedance (Z) parameters** or **hybrid (h) parameters**, which require open or short circuits that are **virtually impossible to realize at RF/microwave frequencies** (you cannot leave an open circuit at 10 GHz — stray capacitance dominates).

At high frequencies, the natural description uses **traveling waves** rather than total voltages and currents. The VNA measures the ratios of these traveling waves.

### 2.1.1 The Two-Port Scattering Framework

For a general **two-port network** (DUT), we define four complex wave quantities:

- **a₁** = Incident wave at Port 1 (forward into the DUT).
- **b₁** = Reflected wave emerging from Port 1.
- **a₂** = Incident wave at Port 2.
- **b₂** = Transmitted/reflected wave emerging from Port 2.

The S-parameter matrix relates them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= S_{11}\,a_1 + S_{12}\,a_2 \\
b_2 &= S_{21}\,a_1 + S_{22}\,a_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.1.2 Operational Definition of Each S-Parameter (The "Why and How")

- **S₁₁ = b₁/a₁ | a₂=0** — *Input Reflection Coefficient*
  - **How:** Terminate Port 2 in a matched load (Z₀), send a signal into Port 1, and measure what comes back.
  - **Why it matters:** Tells you how well the input of the DUT is matched to the system impedance. A perfect match gives S₁₁ = 0.

- **S₂₁ = b₂/a₁ | a₂=0** — *Forward Transmission Coefficient (Gain/Loss)*
  - **How:** Terminate Port 2 in a matched load, send a signal into Port 1, and measure what emerges at Port 2.
  - **Why it matters:** This is the **forward gain** of an amplifier or the **insertion loss** of a filter.

- **S₁₂ = b₁/a₂ | a₁=0** — *Reverse Transmission Coefficient (Isolation)*
  - **How:** Terminate Port 1 in a matched load, send a signal into Port 2, and measure what emerges at Port 1.
  - **Why it matters:** Indicates how much signal leaks backward; critical for amplifiers where reverse isolation prevents oscillation.

- **S₂₂ = b₂/a₂ | a₁=0** — *Output Reflection Coefficient*
  - **How:** Terminate Port 1 in a matched load, send a signal into Port 2, and measure what comes back.
  - **Why it matters:** Output match of the DUT.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reciprocity and Lossless Conditions:**
> - For a **reciprocal** network: **S₂₁ = S₁₂**
> - For a **lossless, matched** network: **|S₁₁|² + |S₂₁|² = 1** (power conservation)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Reflection Coefficient (Γ) | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Dimensionless, complex |
| 2 | S₁₁ in terms of Γ | $S_{11} = \Gamma$ (at Port 1 with Z₀ termination) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Return Loss (RL) | $RL = -20\,\log_{10}\vert S_{11}\vert$ | **dB** (always positive) |
| 4 | Insertion Loss (IL) | $IL = -20\,\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert$ | **dB** (positive for loss) |
| 5 | VSWR | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$ | Dimensionless, $\geq 1$ |
| 6 | Mismatch Loss (ML) | $ML = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^2\right)$ | **dB** |
| 7 | Power Delivered to Load | $P_L = P_{inc}(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^2)$ | **Watts (W)** |
| 8 | Group Delay (τg) | $\tau_g = -\dfrac{1}{360°}\dfrac{d\phi}{df}$ | **seconds** (φ in degrees, f in Hz) |
| 9 | dBm to Voltage Conversion | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{10^{\left(\frac{P_{dBm}}{10}\right)} \cdot 50\,\Omega \cdot 1\,\text{mW}}$ | **Volts** (across 50 Ω) |
| 10 | Reflection Coefficient from RL | $\vert\Gamma\vert = 10^{-RL/20}$ | Dimensionless magnitude |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The VNA is **indispensable** in modern engineering:

- **Antenna Design:** Measuring return loss (S₁₁) of antennas to ensure maximum power transfer and radiation efficiency.
- **Filter Design:** Verifying passband, stopband, ripple, and group delay of RF/microwave filters used in 5G/6G base stations, satellite transponders, and radar systems.
- **Amplifier Characterization:** Measuring S-parameters to compute stability factors, gain, and 1 dB compression points.
- **Material Characterization:** Extracting permittivity (ε) and permeability (μ) of unknown materials placed in a coaxial or waveguide fixture.
- **PCB and Interconnect Validation:** Checking signal integrity on high-speed digital boards (USB 3.0, HDMI, PCIe, SerDes channels).
- **Production Testing:** Mass-production RFIC (Radio-Frequency Integrated Circuit) testing in semiconductor fabs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient and Return Loss

We start with a transmission line of characteristic impedance **Z₀** terminated by a load **Z_L**. We wish to find the relationship between the incident voltage wave **V⁺** and the reflected voltage wave **V⁻** at the load boundary.

### Step 1 — Boundary Condition at the Load

At the load terminal, the total voltage **V** is the sum of incident and reflected waves, and the total current **I** is the difference (because reflected current flows backward):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= V^{+} + V^{-} \\
I &= I^{+} - I^{-} = \frac{V^{+}}{Z_0} - \frac{V^{-}}{Z_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law at the Load

By definition, the load impedance relates the total voltage to the total current:

$$
V = Z_L \cdot I
$$

Substituting the wave expressions:

$$
V^{+} + V^{-} = Z_L \left(\frac{V^{+}}{Z_0} - \frac{V^{-}}{Z_0}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Algebraic Rearrangement

Multiply both sides by **Z₀** to clear the denominator:

$$
Z_0\left(V^{+} + V^{-}\right) = Z_L\left(V^{+} - V^{-}\right)
$$

Expand the right side:

$$
Z_0\,V^{+} + Z_0\,V^{-} = Z_L\,V^{+} - Z_L\,V^{-}
$$

Collect terms with **V⁻** on the left side and terms with **V⁺** on the right side:

$$
Z_0\,V^{-} + Z_L\,V^{-} = Z_L\,V^{+} - Z_0\,V^{+}
$$

Factor out **V⁻** on the left and **V⁺** on the right:

$$
V^{-}(Z_0 + Z_L) = V^{+}(Z_L - Z_0)
$$

### Step 4 — Define and Solve for the Reflection Coefficient

By definition, the reflection coefficient **Γ** is the ratio of the reflected wave to the incident wave at the load:

$$
\Gamma \equiv \frac{V^{-}}{V^{+}}
$$

Substituting into the equation above and solving:

$$
\Gamma = \frac{V^{-}}{V^{+}} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}
$$

This is the foundational relation used in **every** VNA measurement.

### Step 5 — Convert Magnitude to Return Loss (dB)

Return loss in dB is defined as:

$$
RL = 20\,\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\vert\Gamma\vert}\right) = -20\,\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Return Loss uses dB:** Engineers prefer logarithmic units because they compress the enormous dynamic range of RF signals. A return loss of **20 dB** means only **1%** of the incident power is reflected; **10 dB** means **10%** reflected.

## 3.2 Derivation: VSWR in Terms of Reflection Coefficient

The voltage standing wave on a mismatched line oscillates between a maximum and minimum value. Define:

$$
V_{max} = \vert V^{+}\vert + \vert V^{-}\vert, \qquad V_{min} = \vert V^{+}\vert - \vert V^{-}\vert
$$

By definition, VSWR is the ratio of these extrema:

$$
VSWR = \frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} = \frac{\vert V^{+}\vert + \vert V^{-}\vert}{\vert V^{+}\vert - \vert V^{-}\vert}
$$

Now divide numerator and denominator by **|V⁺|**:

$$
VSWR = \frac{1 + \dfrac{\vert V^{-}\vert}{\vert V^{+}\vert}}{1 - \dfrac{\vert V^{-}\vert}{\vert V^{+}\vert}} = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}
$$

This expression has elegant physical meaning:

- When **|Γ| = 0** (perfectly matched line) → **VSWR = 1** (no standing wave).
- When **|Γ| = 1** (open or short circuit) → **VSWR → ∞** (pure standing wave).

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A microwave amplifier is connected to a 50 Ω source through a transmission line. The VNA measures a complex input reflection coefficient of **S₁₁ = 0.30 − j0.40** at the operating frequency. Calculate (a) the magnitude |S₁₁|, (b) the return loss in dB, and (c) the VSWR.

### Part (a): Magnitude of S₁₁

The magnitude of a complex number $z = x + jy$ is $\vert z\vert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$.

$$
\vert S_{11}\vert = \sqrt{(0.30)^2 + (-0.40)^2} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.50
$$

### Part (b): Return Loss in dB

$$
RL = -20\,\log_{10}(0.50) = -20 \times (-0.3010) = 6.02\,\text{dB}
$$

### Part (c): VSWR

$$
VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.50}{1 - 0.50} = \frac{1.50}{0.50} = 3.00
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A return loss of 6.02 dB is **poor** for a communication system (typically we want RL > 15 dB). About **20%** of the input power is reflected back, wasting amplifier efficiency. The VSWR of 3:1 is borderline acceptable and indicates the input matching network needs redesign.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Reflection-Based Metrics

| Metric | Perfect Match | Typical Good | Acceptable | Poor |
|--------|---------------|--------------|------------|------|
| **|Γ|** | 0.00 | 0.05 | 0.18 | 0.50 |
| **RL (dB)** | ∞ | 26.0 dB | 14.9 dB | 6.0 dB |
| **VSWR** | 1.00 : 1 | 1.11 : 1 | 1.44 : 1 | 3.00 : 1 |
| **Reflected Power (%)** | 0 % | 0.25 % | 3.2 % | 25 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern VNA

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal signal-flow architecture of a typical 2-port Vector Network Analyzer, mapping how the stimulus signal is routed, separated, and measured.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Synthesized RF Source Swept Stimulus f_start to f_stop] --> B[Power Splitter]
    B --> C[Reference Channel Receiver R1 measures a1]
    B --> D[S parameter Test Set with Couplers and Switches]
    D --> E[Port 1 Connector to DUT]
    D --> F[Port 2 Connector from DUT]
    E --> G[Test Channel Receiver A measures b1]
    F --> H[Test Channel Receiver B measures b2]
    G --> I[Phase and Magnitude Detector ADC]
    H --> I
    C --> I
    I --> J[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    J --> K[Calibration Correction Engine SOLT SOLR TRL]
    K --> L[Display S parameters Smith Chart Log Mag Group Delay]
    J --> M[User Interface Keyboard Touchscreen]
    L --> N[Output to External PC GPIB USB TCP IP]
```

## 4.2 Calibration Process Flow Diagram

A VNA measurement is only as accurate as its calibration. The following flow chart explains the **SOLT (Short-Open-Load-Thru)** calibration procedure that students will perform in the laboratory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Connect VNA Port 1 and Port 2 to Cal Kit] --> B[Select Calibration Type SOLT]
    B --> C[Measure SHORT standard at Port 1]
    C --> D[Measure OPEN standard at Port 1]
    D --> E[Measure 50 ohm LOAD standard at Port 1]
    E --> F[Repeat SHORT OPEN LOAD at Port 2]
    F --> G[Connect Port 1 to Port 2 directly for THRU]
    G --> H[VNA Computes 12 Term Error Model]
    H --> I[Error Coefficients Stored in Memory]
    I --> J[Disconnect Cal Standards]
    J --> K[Connect DUT and Begin Accurate Measurement]
```

## 4.3 S-Parameter Interpretation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DUT [Two Port Device Under Test]
        P1[Port 1] -->|S11 reflects| P1
        P1 -->|S21 transmits| P2
        P2 -->|S22 reflects| P2
        P2 -->|S12 transmits| P1
    end
    SRC[Matched 50 ohm Source a1] --> P1
    P2 --> LD[Matched 50 ohm Load a2 = 0]
    REF[b1 reflected from Port 1] -->|Measure| VNA[Vector Network Analyzer]
    TRX[b2 transmitted to Port 2] -->|Measure| VNA
    VNA --> DSP[Compute S11 S21 S12 S22 Display]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define Vector Network Analyzer. Why is it called "vector" and not "scalar"?**

**Model Answer:**
A Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) is an RF/microwave test instrument that measures the **complex (magnitude and phase) scattering parameters** of a Device Under Test (DUT) by stimulating the network with a known swept-frequency signal and detecting both the reflected and transmitted waves.

It is called a "**vector**" analyzer because it captures **both amplitude and phase information** of the signal, plotting results as phasors on a complex plane. A *scalar* network analyzer would only measure amplitude (power), losing all phase information critical for impedance, group delay, and time-domain analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **(3 marks allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark vector vs scalar distinction + 1 mark example/application)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is Return Loss? How is it related to the reflection coefficient?**

**Model Answer:**
**Return Loss (RL)** is the ratio (in dB) of the incident power to the reflected power at a port, expressing how well a device is matched to the system impedance **Z₀ = 50 Ω**.

It is related to the reflection coefficient **Γ** by the formula:

$$
RL = -20\,\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert = -20\,\log_{10}\vert S_{11}\vert
$$

A higher return loss (in dB) means a better impedance match. For example, RL = 20 dB implies only 1% of the input power is reflected.

> [!NOTE]
> **(3 marks allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark physical interpretation/example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a Vector Network Analyzer**. Clearly describe the function of the synthesized source, the S-parameter test set, the reference and test receivers, and the calibration engine. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A microstrip filter is measured on a VNA. The measured S₁₁ at 2.4 GHz is **0.158 − j0.196**. Compute (i) the magnitude of S₁₁, (ii) the Return Loss in dB, (iii) the VSWR, and (iv) the percentage of incident power reflected. Comment on the quality of the match. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The VNA architecture consists of the following interconnected functional blocks:

1. **Synthesized RF Source:** Generates a highly stable, phase-locked sinusoidal stimulus that is electronically swept over the user-defined frequency range (e.g., 1 GHz to 6 GHz) with a defined number of points (e.g., 401 or 1601 points). It provides excellent frequency accuracy and phase coherence.

2. **Power Splitter:** Divides the source signal into two paths — one for the reference channel and one for the test port.

3. **S-Parameter Test Set:** The heart of the VNA. It contains directional couplers and an RF switch matrix that routes the stimulus to Port 1 or Port 2 and separates the forward and reverse waves into reference and test channels. Modern VNAs use a 4-channel architecture: a₁, b₁, a₂, b₂.

4. **Reference and Test Receivers:** These are highly sensitive, narrowband heterodyne or ADC-based receivers that down-convert the RF signals to IF, where the **I (in-phase) and Q (quadrature)** components are extracted. This gives both **magnitude and phase** simultaneously.

5. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** Computes the complex ratio `b/a` for each S-parameter and applies the **12-term error correction model** derived from the calibration.

6. **Calibration Engine:** Before any measurement, the VNA measures known calibration standards (Short, Open, Load, Thru) and computes correction coefficients that are stored and applied to subsequent DUT measurements to remove systematic errors (directivity, source match, load match, isolation, frequency response, leakage).

7. **Display / User Interface:** Shows the data in formats like Log Magnitude, Phase, Smith Chart, Polar, Group Delay, or Time Domain.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $S_{11} = 0.158 - j\,0.196$ at $f = 2.4$ GHz.

**(i) Magnitude of S₁₁:**

$$
\vert S_{11}\vert = \sqrt{(0.158)^2 + (-0.196)^2} = \sqrt{0.02496 + 0.03842} = \sqrt{0.06338} \approx 0.2517
$$

**(ii) Return Loss:**

$$
RL = -20\,\log_{10}(0.2517) = -20 \times (-0.5992) \approx 11.98\,\text{dB}
$$

**(iii) VSWR:**

$$
VSWR = \frac{1 + 0.2517}{1 - 0.2517} = \frac{1.2517}{0.7483} \approx 1.673 : 1
$$

**(iv) Percentage of Power Reflected:**

$$
\text{Reflected Power \%} = \vert S_{11}\vert^2 \times 100 = (0.2517)^2 \times 100 \approx 6.33\,\%
$$

**Comment on Match Quality:** A return loss of ~12 dB indicates an **acceptable but not excellent** match (industry standard for many systems is RL ≥ 15 dB). About **6.33%** of the incident power is reflected back, slightly degrading efficiency. The VSWR of 1.67:1 is reasonable for a filter at its band-edge frequency but indicates room for matching improvement.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**a)** Explain the **SOLT calibration procedure** of a VNA in detail. What systematic errors are corrected by this calibration? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A microwave amplifier has the following S-parameters measured at 3 GHz: **S₁₁ = 0.20 ∠ −45°**, **S₂₁ = 5.0 ∠ 120°**, **S₁₂ = 0.05 ∠ 60°**, **S₂₂ = 0.30 ∠ −30°**. Determine (i) the forward power gain in dB, (ii) the input return loss in dB, (iii) the output return loss in dB, and (iv) the reverse isolation in dB. Is the amplifier unilateral? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**SOLT = Short-Open-Load-Thru** is the most common coaxial VNA calibration method.

**Procedure:**
1. **Mechanical Calibration Kit** with precisely defined standards (Short, Open, Load, Thru) is connected to the VNA test ports.
2. The user selects **SOLT calibration** in the VNA menu and sets the frequency range and number of points.
3. **Port 1 Calibration:** The Short, Open, and Load standards are connected sequentially to Port 1 (with Port 2 terminated in the Load standard).
4. **Port 2 Calibration:** Same process repeated at Port 2.
5. **THRU connection:** Port 1 and Port 2 are connected directly with a precision thru cable or adapter.
6. The VNA measures the actual response of each standard, compares it with the mathematically defined ideal response (known from the cal kit's `.csv` definition file), and computes the **12 error coefficients** of its internal error model.
7. The error coefficients are stored in volatile memory and applied as a correction matrix to all subsequent DUT measurements.

**Systematic Errors Corrected:**
- **Directivity Error** (leakage in directional couplers)
- **Source Match Error** (imperfect match of the source)
- **Load Match Error** (imperfect termination of the test port)
- **Isolation Error** (crosstalk between channels)
- **Frequency Response Errors** (amplitude and phase flatness of the receivers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without calibration, raw VNA measurements can have **±1 to 2 dB magnitude error** and **±5° to 10° phase error**. With proper SOLT, these reduce to **±0.05 dB** and **±0.5°**, respectively.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given S-parameters (complex form):**

First, extract the magnitudes:
- $|S_{11}| = 0.20$
- $|S_{21}| = 5.0$
- $|S_{12}| = 0.05$
- $|S_{22}| = 0.30$

**(i) Forward Power Gain:**

$$
G_{forward}\,[\text{dB}] = 20\,\log_{10}\vert S_{21}\vert = 20\,\log_{10}(5.0) = 20 \times 0.6990 = 13.98\,\text{dB}
$$

**(ii) Input Return Loss:**

$$
RL_{in} = -20\,\log_{10}\vert S_{11}\vert = -20\,\log_{10}(0.20) = -20 \times (-0.6990) = 13.98\,\text{dB}
$$

**(iii) Output Return Loss:**

$$
RL_{out} = -20\,\log_{10}\vert S_{22}\vert = -20\,\log_{10}(0.30) = -20 \times (-0.5229) = 10.46\,\text{dB}
$$

**(iv) Reverse Isolation:**

$$
ISO_{rev}\,[\text{dB}] = -20\,\log_{10}\vert S_{12}\vert = -20\,\log_{10}(0.05) = -20 \times (-1.3010) = 26.02\,\text{dB}
$$

**Unilaterality Check:**

A network is **unilateral** if $S_{12} = 0$ (no reverse transmission). In practice, we check the **unilateral figure of merit**:

$$
U = \frac{\vert S_{11}\vert\,\vert S_{12}\vert\,\vert S_{22}\vert}{(1 - \vert S_{11}\vert^2)(1 - \vert S_{22}\vert^2)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
U = \frac{(0.20)(0.05)(0.30)}{(1 - 0.04)(1 - 0.09)} = \frac{0.003}{0.96 \times 0.91} = \frac{0.003}{0.8736} \approx 0.00343
$$

Since $U \ll 0.1$, the amplifier is **effectively unilateral** (practically, $|S_{12}|$ is very small compared to $|S_{21}|$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Return Loss is **positive** in dB — students often forget the negative sign in $-20\,\log_{10}|\Gamma|$ and end up with negative dB values. Always cross-check: a matched line MUST have RL = ∞ dB, not $-\infty$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Termination Condition:** When defining S₁₁ and S₂₁, students write the formula but forget to mention the condition "$a_2 = 0$ (Port 2 terminated in matched load)." This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Mixing dB and Linear Values:** Insertion/Return loss in dB must be converted back to linear ratio using $10^{x/20}$ (for voltages/field quantities) or $10^{x/10}$ (for power quantities) before further calculation. Students often use the wrong factor.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Phase in Smith Chart Questions:** When plotting on a Smith chart, students plot only the magnitude and lose 2-3 marks for omitting the phase angle.
>
> 5. **VSWR Formula Inversion:** Some students write $VSWR = (1 - |\Gamma|)/(1 + |\Gamma|)$, inverting the numerator and denominator. Always remember: numerator is **1 + |Γ|**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **VNA = Vector (Magnitude + Phase) Network Analyzer** — measures complex S-parameters of RF/microwave networks.
- [ ] **S-parameters** use traveling wave variables: $S_{ij} = b_i / a_j$ (with all other ports matched).
- [ ] **Reference Impedance** is always **Z₀ = 50 Ω** (75 Ω for video systems).
- [ ] **S₁₁** = Input Reflection Coefficient → directly gives **Return Loss** in dB.
- [ ] **S₂₁** = Forward Transmission → gives **Insertion Loss / Gain** in dB.
- [ ] **S₂₂** = Output Reflection Coefficient → gives **Output Match**.
- [ ] **S₁₂** = Reverse Transmission → gives **Reverse Isolation**.
- [ ] **Reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$.
- [ ] **Return Loss:** $RL = -20\,\log_{10}|\Gamma| \geq 0$ dB.
- [ ] **VSWR:** $(1+|\Gamma|)/(1-|\Gamma|)$, ranges from 1 to $\infty$.
- [ ] **Power conservation** for lossless matched two-port: $|S_{11}|^2 + |S_{21}|^2 = 1$.
- [ ] **SOLT calibration** is mandatory for accurate measurements; it removes 6 systematic error terms (directivity, isolation, source match, load match, frequency response for both reflection and transmission).
- [ ] **Smith Chart** is a polar plot of complex $\Gamma$ normalized to 50 Ω; constant resistance circles and constant reactance arcs.
- [ ] **Group Delay** $\tau_g = -(1/360°)(d\phi/df)$ reveals signal distortion in wideband systems.
- [ ] **VNA application domains:** Antenna design, filter design, amplifier characterization, material measurement, PCB signal integrity, production testing of RFICs.
- [ ] **Typical lab exercise:** Calibrate VNA with SOLT kit → measure S₁₁ of an antenna → measure S₂₁ of a filter → plot on Smith chart and log-mag display.
