# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Antenna Pattern Measurement** is the experimental procedure used in antenna engineering to determine the **radiation pattern** of an Antenna Under Test (AUT) by recording the variation of received signal strength (or radiated field intensity) as a function of angular position around the antenna, while maintaining a constant distance under far-field (Fraunhofer) conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> The radiation pattern of an antenna is a graphical representation (mathematical function or spatial distribution) of the **far-field radiation properties** of the antenna as a function of **spatial coordinates** (θ, ϕ). Pattern measurement is the standardized laboratory experiment to plot this distribution using a controlled RF test range.

The standard measurement plot produces two principal cuts:
- **E-Plane Pattern**: The radiation pattern in the plane containing the **Electric field vector** and the **direction of maximum radiation**.
- **H-Plane Pattern**: The radiation pattern in the plane containing the **Magnetic field vector** and the **direction of maximum radiation**, orthogonal to the E-plane.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Torchlight" Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a dark room holding a torch (flashlight). The torch is the **Antenna**, and the room is the **3D space** around it.

- If you walk in a circle around yourself while someone measures the brightness of the torch's beam at every angle, the data you record forms the **Radiation Pattern**.
- The bright main spot of the torch = **Main Lobe** (direction of maximum radiation).
- The faint glow leaking out the back of the torch = **Back Lobe**.
- Small dim rays leaking from the side of the bulb = **Side Lobes**.
- The torch's brightness drops sharply outside the main beam = **Beamwidth**.

Now imagine you do this entire experiment while standing **far enough** from the observer so that the beam becomes parallel (not spreading too quickly). That "far enough" distance is the **Fraunhofer Distance** — and it's exactly what defines a valid antenna pattern measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we measure patterns:** Engineers must know *where* the antenna radiates most strongly, *how focused* the beam is, and *how much power leaks* into unwanted directions — these dictate cell tower placement, radar cross-section, satellite link alignment, and 5G MIMO beamforming.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **AUT** | Antenna Under Test (the device being characterized) |
| **HPBW** | Half-Power Beamwidth (angular width at 3 dB points) |
| **FNBW** | First-Null Beamwidth (angular width between first nulls) |
| **SLL** | Side Lobe Level (in dB, relative to main lobe peak) |
| **BWFN** | Beamwidth between First Nulls (synonym of FNBW) |
| **Far-Field** | Region where field varies as 1/R and angular pattern is independent of R |
| **Test Range** | The open indoor/outdoor facility used to perform the measurement |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Experiment

The experiment follows a strict sequence of physical and procedural rules. Each step is non-negotiable for a valid KTU lab record.

### Step-by-Step Experimental Logic

1. **Select the test frequency** (f in MHz or GHz). This determines the operating wavelength **λ = c/f**, where **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** is the speed of light.
2. **Compute the largest dimension D** of the AUT (e.g., for a dipole, D = length L; for a horn, D = aperture diagonal).
3. **Verify the Fraunhofer (Far-Field) distance condition** so that the receiving antenna sees a planar (locally) wavefront from the AUT.
4. **Set up the RF Test Range** with three essential blocks: RF Source → Transmitting Antenna, free-space propagation path, and the **AUT mounted on a Positioner** (rotating tower) connected to a Receiver (Spectrum Analyzer / Power Meter).
5. **Calibrate** the system: record received power when the AUT is aligned (θ = 0°) with the source antenna. This is the **0 dB reference** for the pattern.
6. **Rotate the AUT** in discrete angular steps (typically every 2° or 5°), recording the received power at each step over a full 360° rotation.
7. **Normalize** the recorded data to the maximum value (0 dB) and plot the pattern in **rectangular (Cartesian)** or **polar** form.
8. **Extract beam parameters**: HPBW, FNBW, SLL, front-to-back ratio, directivity.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why far-field?** In the near field, the pattern shape changes with distance — so two engineers measuring the same antenna at different distances would record *different* patterns. The far-field guarantees a unique, distance-independent pattern.
- **Why rotate the AUT and not the source?** The source antenna is typically a high-gain reference horn whose pattern is already well-characterized. By keeping it fixed and rotating only the AUT, the source's pattern becomes a known "illumination" that we mathematically de-embed.
- **Why normalize?** A pattern describes *shape*, not absolute power. Normalization lets us compare antennas of different gains on a single 0 dB scale.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the formulas below. They appear in **every KTU university exam and lab viva** for this experiment.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength from operating frequency | meters (m) |
| 2 | $R_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^{2}}{\lambda}$ | **Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Distance** (standard criterion) | meters (m) |
| 3 | $R_{ff} = \dfrac{0.5 D^{2}}{\lambda}$ | Reactive near-field boundary (sometimes cited) | meters (m) |
| 4 | $\text{Normalized Power (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{r}(\theta)}{P_{max}}\right)$ | Pattern normalization in decibels | dB |
| 5 | $\text{HPBW (rad)} \approx \dfrac{0.886 \,\lambda}{D}$ | HPBW for a uniformly illuminated aperture (in radians) | rad |
| 6 | $\text{HPBW (deg)} \approx \dfrac{51 \,\lambda}{D}$ | HPBW for a uniformly illuminated aperture (in degrees) | degrees |
| 7 | $D_{0} \approx \dfrac{41253}{\text{HPBW}_{E} \cdot \text{HPBW}_{H}}$ | Approximate directivity from beamwidths (degrees) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $G = \eta \cdot D_{0}$ | Gain = efficiency × directivity | dimensionless (dBi) |
| 9 | $\text{F/B ratio} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{forward}}{P_{backward}}\right)$ | Front-to-Back ratio (dB) | dB |
| 10 | $P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$ | Friis Transmission Equation (governs link power) | W (watts) |

**Boundary / Validity Conditions:**
- The measurement range **R must satisfy R ≥ 2D²/λ** (Fraunhofer).
- The AUT must be in the **far-field** of *both* the source antenna and the positioner tower.
- Free-space path loss is valid only when no significant ground bounce or multipath corrupts the main beam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of this Experiment:**
> - In **5G/6G base-station design**, HPBW determines sector coverage and cell overlap.
> - In **radar systems**, SLL and F/B ratio determine target detection in cluttered environments.
> - In **satellite communication**, the E/H plane patterns decide whether an antenna on a moving platform (aircraft/ship) loses lock on the GEO satellite.
> - In **EMI/EMC compliance testing**, side-lobe leakage can cause unwanted coupling between adjacent systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Distance

The far-field criterion is derived from the maximum allowable **quadratic phase error** of $\pi/8$ radians (≈ 22.5°) across the antenna aperture. The result is the universally accepted **2D²/λ** rule.

**Starting point:** Consider the AUT as an aperture of largest linear dimension **D**. A wave traveling from the edge of the aperture to a far-field observation point at distance **R** travels a longer path than a wave from the center of the aperture.

The path-length difference between edge and center is:

$$\Delta R = R - \sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

Expanding the square root using the binomial series for $R \gg D$:

$$\sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2}} = R\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{D}{2R}\right)^{2}} \approx R - \dfrac{D^{2}}{8R}$$

Substituting back:

$$\Delta R \approx \dfrac{D^{2}}{8R}$$

The corresponding phase difference (in radians) is:

$$\Delta \phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta R = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{D^{2}}{8R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4 \lambda R}$$

The Fraunhofer condition demands this phase error not exceed $\pi/8$ rad:

$$\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4 \lambda R} \le \dfrac{\pi}{8}$$

Multiplying both sides by $4\lambda R / \pi$ and simplifying:

$$D^{2} \le \dfrac{\lambda R}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R \ge \dfrac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda}$$

Therefore, the **minimum measurement distance** is:

$$\boxed{R_{ff} = \dfrac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda}}$$

This is the result the KTU board expects you to write in any calculation-based viva or exam question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Normalized Power Pattern (dB)

Pattern data is recorded as received power $P_r(\theta)$ at each angular step. The **normalized** value in dB is:

$$P_{n}(\theta)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{r}(\theta)}{P_{max}}\right)$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- The numerator $P_r(\theta)$ is the received power at angle $\theta$.
- The denominator $P_{max}$ is the maximum received power (occurring at the main-beam peak).
- The ratio is a pure number between 0 and 1.
- Multiplying by 10 (not 20, because power not field) and taking $\log_{10}$ converts it to decibels.
- The result is always $\le 0$ dB, with the main beam peak anchored at **0 dB**.

**Numerical worked example:**

Suppose the recorded powers at angles 0°, 30°, 60°, 90° are 100, 50, 12, 1 μW. The normalized dB values are:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_n(0^{\circ}) &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{100}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1) = 0 \text{ dB} \\
P_n(30^{\circ}) &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{50}{100}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(0.5) = -3.01 \text{ dB} \\
P_n(60^{\circ}) &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{12}{100}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(0.12) = -9.21 \text{ dB} \\
P_n(90^{\circ}) &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{100}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(0.01) = -20.00 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

Notice that the **−3 dB point** lies between 0° and 30° (the half-power beamwidth boundary). The **−20 dB point at 90°** indicates the back-lobe level (front-to-back ratio ≈ 20 dB).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — HPBW Approximation for an Aperture Antenna

For a uniformly illuminated rectangular aperture of dimension $D$ (in one plane), the far-field pattern is a sinc function. The 3 dB (half-power) width of the main lobe is given by the well-known textbook result:

$$\text{HPBW}_{\text{rad}} \approx \dfrac{0.886 \, \lambda}{D}$$

Converting to degrees (multiply by $180/\pi$):

$$\text{HPBW}_{\text{deg}} \approx \dfrac{0.886 \cdot 180}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{D} \approx \dfrac{50.8 \, \lambda}{D} \approx \dfrac{51 \, \lambda}{D}$$

This is the **directivity-controlled** beamwidth: the larger the aperture $D$ in wavelengths, the narrower the beam.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Formula

| Scenario in the Question | Use This Formula |
|---|---|
| "Compute minimum distance between source and AUT" | $R_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^{2}}{\lambda}$ |
| "Convert received power to normalized dB pattern" | $P_n(\theta) = 10 \log_{10}(P_r(\theta)/P_{max})$ |
| "Find HPBW of an aperture antenna" | $\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{51 \lambda}{D}$ degrees |
| "Estimate directivity from measured beamwidths" | $D_0 \approx \dfrac{41253}{\text{HPBW}_E \cdot \text{HPBW}_H}$ |
| "Compute link power for given R, f, gains" | Friis: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^{2}$ |
| "Find F/B ratio from forward and backward powers" | $F/B = 10 \log_{10}(P_{fwd}/P_{back})$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — RF Test Range Setup

The following Mermaid diagram describes the **functional architecture of the antenna pattern measurement test range** as it is implemented in a typical KTU-affiliated microwave/antenna lab. It traces the **signal flow** from source to recorded data.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RFSource["RF Signal Generator"] --> TxAmp["Power Amplifier"]
    TxAmp --> TxAnt["Transmitting Horn Antenna"]
    TxAnt -->|"Free Space R >= 2D^2 by lambda"| AUTmount["AUT on Rotating Positioner Tower"]
    AUTmount --> RxFeed["RF Coaxial Feed Line"]
    RxFeed --> Detector["Detector or Down-Converter"]
    Detector --> PowerMeter["Power Meter or Spectrum Analyzer"]
    PowerMeter --> DAQ["Data Acquisition PC"]
    DAQ --> Plotter["Pattern Plot Rectangular or Polar"]
    PositionerCtrl["Positioner Controller Stepper Motor"] --> AUTmount
    DAQ --> PositionerCtrl
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **left side** of the test range is the *illumination* subsystem (signal source → transmitting antenna).
- The **middle block** is the **AUT mounted on the rotating positioner** — this is what rotates in discrete angular steps.
- The **right side** captures the received signal, digitizes it, and plots the pattern.
- The **bidirectional link** between the DAQ and the positioner controller indicates *closed-loop* angular stepping: the PC commands the positioner to move to angle $\theta$, the AUT settles, the power is measured, then the next angle is commanded.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define Test Frequency f and Wavelength lambda"] --> B["Measure AUT Largest Dimension D"]
    B --> C["Compute Fraunhofer Distance Rff = 2D^2 by lambda"]
    C --> D["Set Up Test Range with R greater than or equal to Rff"]
    D --> E["Align AUT Main Lobe with Tx Antenna at theta = 0"]
    E --> F["Record Reference Power Pmax"]
    F --> G{"For each theta in 0 to 360 step delta theta"}
    G --> H["Rotate AUT to angle theta via Positioner"]
    H --> I["Record Received Power Pr at this theta"]
    I --> J["Compute Normalized dB: 10 log Pr by Pmax"]
    J --> K["Store theta and Pn in Data Table"]
    K --> G
    G --> L["End Loop: Full 360 degree Sweep Complete"]
    L --> M["Plot Pattern: theta on x axis, Pn dB on y axis"]
    M --> N["Extract HPBW FNBW SLL F to B from Plot"]
    N --> O["Compare with Theoretical Pattern if available"]
    O --> P["Stop: Report Submitted"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Coordinate System Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AUT["AUT at Origin"] --- Eplane["E-Plane: Contains E vector and Direction of Max Radiation"]
    AUT --- Hplane["H-Plane: Contains H vector, Orthogonal to E-Plane"]
    Eplane -->|"Pattern cut 1"| Eplot["E-Plane Polar Plot"]
    Hplane -->|"Pattern cut 2"| Hplot["H-Plane Polar Plot"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Tip:** When asked "which plane is being measured?", answer: *"The plane in which we rotate the AUT's E (or H) field vector."* For a standard half-wave dipole (vertical), the E-plane is the *vertical* plane and the H-plane is the *horizontal* plane.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define radiation pattern of an antenna. What is the difference between E-plane and H-plane patterns?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The radiation pattern of an antenna is a mathematical function or graphical representation of the far-zone field intensity (or power) radiated by the antenna as a function of the spherical angular coordinates $(\theta, \phi)$ at a fixed radial distance $R$ satisfying the Fraunhofer condition.

- **E-Plane Pattern:** The pattern measured in the plane containing the **electric field vector** and the **direction of maximum radiation**. For a vertically oriented dipole, this is the elevation (vertical) cut.
- **H-Plane Pattern:** The pattern measured in the plane containing the **magnetic field vector** (orthogonal to E) and the **direction of maximum radiation**. For the same dipole, this is the azimuthal (horizontal) cut.

The E-plane and H-plane patterns are always **orthogonal** planes in the spherical coordinate system centered on the AUT.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**State the Fraunhofer distance criterion. Why is it essential in antenna pattern measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Fraunhofer distance, also called the far-field distance, is the **minimum radial distance** from the antenna at which the far-field pattern becomes independent of distance and the wavefront is effectively planar across the antenna aperture.

$$R_{ff} = \dfrac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda}$$

where $D$ is the largest dimension of the AUT and $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength.

**Why it is essential:** In the near-field region (Reactive and Radiative near-field zones), the angular distribution of the field depends strongly on the observation distance. Measurement at any $R < R_{ff}$ would yield a *distance-dependent* and *phase-error-corrupted* pattern, leading to incorrect beamwidths, side-lobe levels, and null positions. The Fraunhofer criterion guarantees a **unique, repeatable, and physically meaningful** radiation pattern for the AUT.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer both Part A and Part B. In Part B, answer ANY ONE full question from the two alternatives.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **experimental setup** used to measure the radiation pattern of an antenna in the laboratory. List the **essential instruments** required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard gain horn antenna with aperture dimension $D = 15$ cm is to be measured at an operating frequency of **10 GHz**. Compute:
  - (i) the operating wavelength $\lambda$,
  - (ii) the minimum far-field distance $R_{ff}$,
  - (iii) the approximate HPBW in degrees (assuming uniform illumination).

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram of the RF Test Range:**

```
[RF Signal Generator] → [Variable Attenuator] → [Power Amplifier]
        ↓
[Transmitting Horn Antenna] ──── Free Space (R ≥ 2D²/λ) ────→ [AUT on Positioner]
                                                                            ↓
                              [Power Meter] ← [Detector] ← [Coaxial Feed]
```

**Instruments Required (list with brief role):**
1. **RF Signal Generator** (e.g., HP 8648, 9 kHz – 3 GHz) — provides the CW test tone.
2. **Power Amplifier** — boosts the signal to a level that overcomes free-space path loss.
3. **Transmitting Horn / Standard Gain Horn** — illuminates the AUT with a known, stable field.
4. **AUT mounted on a Positioner (rotating tower with stepper motor)** — rotates in precise angular increments.
5. **Detector (Crystal Detector / Schottky diode)** — converts RF to baseband/DC proportional to received power.
6. **Power Meter / Spectrum Analyzer** — quantifies the received signal level in dBm.
7. **Positioner Controller + Data Acquisition Software** — automates the rotation and logs power vs. angle.
8. **Coaxial Cables, Waveguides, Connectors** — RF plumbing.
9. **Anechoic Chamber or Open Range** — suppresses multipath/ground reflections.

**Procedure (summarized):**
- Mount the AUT on the positioner at height equal to the transmitting antenna's center.
- Verify $R \ge R_{ff} = 2D^2/\lambda$.
- Sweep $\theta$ from $0°$ to $360°$ in $2°$ or $5°$ steps.
- Record received power, normalize, plot the pattern.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $D = 15$ cm $= 0.15$ m, $f = 10$ GHz $= 10 \times 10^9$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Operating wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Minimum far-field distance:**

$$R_{ff} = \dfrac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 \times (0.15)^{2}}{0.03} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.0225}{0.03} = \dfrac{0.045}{0.03} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Approximate HPBW (uniform illumination, in degrees):**

$$\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{51 \, \lambda}{D} = \dfrac{51 \times 0.03}{0.15} = \dfrac{1.53}{0.15} = 10.2^{\circ}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\lambda = 3$ cm
- (ii) $R_{ff} = 1.5$ m
- (iii) $\text{HPBW} \approx 10.2°$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with reference to an antenna radiation pattern:
  - (i) Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW)
  - (ii) First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW)
  - (iii) Side Lobe Level (SLL)
  - (iv) Front-to-Back (F/B) ratio

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a pattern measurement, the following data is recorded (received power in μW vs. angle in degrees). The maximum power is **500 μW at 0°**.

| θ (°) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 90 | 180 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_r$ (μW) | 500 | 480 | 250 | 125 | 40 | 8 | 1.5 | 0.5 | 5 |

Compute the **HPBW**, the **F/B ratio (in dB)**, and identify which angles represent the **half-power points** and the **first nulls**.

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW):** The angular separation between the two directions in a pattern cut on which the radiated (or received) power is **half (−3 dB)** of the maximum value of the main lobe. It is the most common figure-of-merit for antenna *directivity*: narrower HPBW ⇒ higher directivity.

**(ii) First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW):** Also called BWFN. The angular separation between the **first nulls** (or minima) of the radiation pattern on either side of the main-lobe peak. FNBW is always **greater than** HPBW for the same antenna.

**(iii) Side Lobe Level (SLL):** The ratio (in dB) of the **largest side-lobe magnitude** to the **main-lobe peak magnitude**, both measured in the radiation pattern.

$$\text{SLL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_{\text{side-lobe max}}}{E_{\text{main-lobe max}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{side-lobe max}}}{P_{\text{main-lobe max}}}\right)$$

A *negative* SLL is normal (side lobes are always weaker than the main lobe). Lower SLL is desirable in radar/communication systems.

**(iv) Front-to-Back (F/B) Ratio:** The ratio (in dB) of the **power radiated in the forward (main-beam) direction** to the **power radiated in the exact opposite (180°) direction**.

$$F/B = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{fwd}}{P_{180°}}\right)$$

A high F/B ratio means the antenna rejects signals arriving from behind, which is critical in point-to-point links, radar, and radio astronomy.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P_{max} = 500$ μW at $\theta = 0°$.

**Step 1: Compute half-power level.**

$$P_{HP} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2} = \dfrac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ μW} \quad (-3 \text{ dB point})$$

**Step 2: Locate half-power points from the table.**

At $\theta = 0°$, $P_r = 500$ μW (peak).
At $\theta = 20°$, $P_r = 250$ μW ← **exactly half** ⇒ this is a **half-power point**.

The pattern is symmetric, so by inspection of the given data, the other half-power point is at $\theta = -20°$ (i.e., $340°$ in 0–360 range).

$$\boxed{\text{HPBW} = 20^{\circ} - (-20^{\circ}) = 40^{\circ}}$$

**Step 3: Identify the first nulls.**

A *null* is the local minimum between the main lobe and the first side lobe. From the table:
- $P_r$ drops from 500 (at 0°) → 250 (at 20°) → 40 (at 40°) → 8 (at 50°) → 1.5 (at 60°).
- The minimum in this region is at $\theta = 60°$ ($P_r = 1.5$ μW) — this is the **first null**.

By symmetry, the first null on the other side is at $\theta = -60°$ (or $300°$).

$$\boxed{\text{FNBW} = 60^{\circ} - (-60^{\circ}) = 120^{\circ}}$$

**Step 4: Compute Front-to-Back ratio (in dB).**

From the table, at $\theta = 180°$, $P_r = 5$ μW. The forward maximum is at $\theta = 0°$ with $P_r = 500$ μW.

$$F/B = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{fwd}}{P_{180°}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{500}{5}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \text{ dB}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Half-power points: $\theta = \pm 20°$
- First nulls: $\theta = \pm 60°$
- $\text{HPBW} = 40°$
- $\text{FNBW} = 120°$
- $F/B = 20$ dB

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $D$ must be in meters (not cm) and $\lambda$ must be in the same units. Mixing cm and m is the #1 calculation error in KTU lab exams.
> 2. **Using 20·log instead of 10·log:** For *power ratios* (received power pattern), use $10 \log_{10}$. For *field (voltage) ratios*, use $20 \log_{10}$. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not specifying E-plane or H-plane:** A pattern plot is meaningless without labeling which plane it represents. Always mention "E-plane cut" or "H-plane cut."
> 4. **Confusing HPBW with FNBW:** HPBW is between **−3 dB points**; FNBW is between **first nulls** (typically −20 to −30 dB). They are *not* the same.
> 5. **Forgetting the −3 dB justification in HPBW:** A correct answer should explicitly state "the points where the power drops to half (−3 dB) of its maximum value."
> 6. **Drawing an unnormalized pattern:** Plots must be **normalized to 0 dB at the peak** for proper pattern interpretation. Absolute dBm plots are not radiation patterns.
> 7. **Omitting the far-field verification:** If a question gives you a distance, always verify $R \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ *first* before doing any further calculation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Radiation Pattern** = spatial variation of far-field intensity vs. angle $(\theta, \phi)$ at constant $R$.
- [x] **Two principal cuts** are measured: **E-plane** and **H-plane** (orthogonal planes).
- [x] **Fraunhofer distance** $R_{ff} = 2D^2/\lambda$ is the **minimum** valid measurement distance.
- [x] **HPBW** is measured at the **−3 dB points** (half-power) on either side of the main lobe peak.
- [x] **FNBW** is measured at the **first nulls** on either side of the main lobe.
- [x] **SLL (dB)** is always **negative** — it is the ratio of side-lobe peak to main-lobe peak in dB.
- [x] **F/B ratio (dB)** is the forward-to-backward power ratio in dB; higher is better.
- [x] **Normalize** the pattern to 0 dB at the peak before plotting.
- [x] Use **$10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios**; **$20 \log_{10}$ for field ratios**.
- [x] **Far-field condition is mandatory** — invalidates the entire experiment if violated.
- [x] For a uniformly illuminated aperture, $\text{HPBW} \approx 51\lambda/D$ degrees.
- [x] Friis equation governs the link power received by the AUT during the test.
- [x] **Antenna is reciprocal** — a receiving pattern equals the transmitting pattern (used implicitly in measurement).
- [x] Standard lab instruments: **RF generator, source antenna, AUT on positioner, detector, power meter/spectrum analyzer, DAQ system**.
- [x] **Plot either in rectangular (θ on x-axis) or polar (θ as angle) — both are acceptable in KTU records.**
- [x] Always mention the **plane of measurement** (E or H) and the **frequency of operation** in the heading of the plot.
